Use personal, non-client work (her painted maps) as a lab to push typographic ideas with no constraints

sections.name: How she begins
sections.notes: Starts from the gut, not from a brief-driven options matrix. The Citibank mark was drawn in seconds on a napkin in the first meeting — the answer arrived whole, then the agency spent months rationalizing what she already knew. She trusts the immediate instinct as the real design and treats the explanation as a later, separate job.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Design first, justify second. She makes a decisive intuitive choice, then reverse-engineers the logic and language that defends it to clients and to herself. She frames the famous 'it took me a few seconds and 34 years' idea: the instant solution is actually the payoff of decades of immersion, so speed is earned expertise, not luck.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Type as image. She abandons type-as-quiet-information and uses dense, layered, scaled wood-type and lettering as the entire visual field — the Public Theater identity is built almost purely from active typography. Her painted typographic maps push this furthest: words and place-names become texture, color, and surface, blurring graphic design and fine art.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Commit boldly and let the system do the work across contexts. Build identities flexible and loud enough to live on posters, signage, and full environments without re-solving each application. Keep a personal practice (the maps) running alongside client work as a pressure-free space to evolve ideas. Believe that depth of experience, not deliberation in the moment, produces the fast right answer.

principle.title: Decide on instinct, defend with logic after
principle.explanation: Scher's best-known marks came from an immediate gut decision, with the rational justification assembled afterward — because the instinct was itself the product of deep, long-accumulated expertise. Waiting for analysis to authorize the move only dilutes the conviction that made it strong.
principle.howToApply: On a new design problem, make your real choice fast and on feel, then separately write the rationale that explains why it works. If you can't justify it, refine it — but don't let the absence of an upfront rationale stop you from committing to the instinctive answer first.

entries.tagline: Drawing is thinking made visible — and art is work.
entries.summary: Milton Glaser was the American designer behind the I♥NY logo and the rainbow-haired Bob Dylan poster, and co-founder of Push Pin Studios and New York Magazine. He rejected the rigid orthodoxy of Swiss modernism in favor of an eclectic, historically literate, drawing-led practice that borrowed freely from any era or style the idea demanded. For Glaser, design was less about applying a fixed system and more about understanding a problem deeply enough to make a fresh, surprising response. His twin mottos — 'Art is Work' and 'drawing is thinking' — frame design as disciplined labor and as a way of seeing.
whatWorks: Sketch by hand first — use drawing to think through the problem, not just to render a decided answer
Refuse a house style; choose the visual language each problem actually needs, pulling from any period or tradition
Make the familiar strange so a tired subject becomes worth looking at again
Treat design as a tool for understanding — research and absorb the subject before forming a solution
Reduce to the irreducible idea (a heart, a silhouette) so the concept carries the work, not the decoration
Treat it as work: show up, iterate, and accept that good outcomes come from sustained effort, not waiting for inspiration

sections.name: How he begins
sections.notes: Starts with the pencil, not the grid. Glaser drew to think — sketching loosely to discover the idea rather than to execute a predetermined one. The hand leads the mind; the act of drawing surfaces possibilities that analysis alone would miss.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Understand first, then respond. He immersed himself in the subject and in the whole history of visual culture, then chose the style the specific problem demanded — eclectic and anti-dogmatic, never a single signature look imposed from outside. Design was a means of understanding the world, so comprehension preceded form.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Synthesis of high and low, old and new: Art Nouveau curves, Islamic pattern, comic-strip directness, psychedelic color — recombined into something legible and new. He distilled concepts to a single irreducible mark (I♥NY's rebus; Dylan's silhouette + rainbow hair).

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: 'Make the familiar strange' — defamiliarize the everyday so people truly see it again. 'Art is Work' — outcomes come from disciplined effort, not muses. 'Drawing is thinking.' Reject orthodoxy and dogma; stay curious, keep learning, let the idea (not the system) govern the execution.

principle.title: Draw to think, then choose the style the idea needs
principle.explanation: Glaser used the pencil as a thinking tool and refused a fixed house style, so each solution fit its problem and stayed fresh — the opposite of forcing every brief through one system.
principle.howToApply: Before opening design software, sketch the idea by hand to externalize and test it. Then ask what visual language this particular problem demands — and let that, not your default toolkit, drive the execution. Distill to the single irreducible mark, and find one move that makes the familiar subject strange enough to be seen anew.

entries.tagline: Concept first, craft by hand, and close the studio every seven years to refill the well.
entries.summary: Stefan Sagmeister is an Austrian-born, New York-based designer best known for album packaging (Talking Heads, Lou Reed, the Rolling Stones), the legendary AIGA lecture poster with the talk's details carved into his own torso, and the ongoing 'Things I Have Learned in My Life So Far' project. With partner Jessica Walsh he ran Sagmeister & Walsh, pursuing a deeply conceptual, handmade practice that treats the idea — not the execution — as the hard part. He is famous for closing the studio entirely for a year-long sabbatical every seven years, a discipline he credits as the single biggest driver of the work's originality, and for the data-rich 'Beauty' and 'Now Is Better' projects late in his career.
whatWorks: Keep a running diary of personal truths and observations, then mine it for project content instead of inventing concepts on demand
Lock the concept before touching craft — decide what the piece must *say*, then ask what physical form proves it
Make it by hand: build, carve, photograph, or stage the idea in the real world rather than defaulting to the screen
Schedule deliberate fallow time (his year-every-seven sabbatical) as a non-negotiable input to creative quality, not a reward
Let discomfort or even pain be in service of the idea — willing to be the medium when it makes the message undeniable
Use long-horizon, evolving projects to test one question many ways over years instead of one-off deliverables

sections.name: How he begins
sections.notes: Starts from a personal diary of 'things I have learned' — honest, sometimes banal life-truths logged over years. Ideas are pulled from lived observation rather than mood boards, so the content is authentic before any visual form exists. The brief becomes: what do I actually believe, and how do I make that physical?

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Concept-first and emphatically handmade. He fixes the idea before the aesthetic, then realizes it through real-world fabrication — carving, building giant installations, arranging objects, staging photography. The computer is a finishing tool, not where ideas are born; the labor of making by hand is part of the meaning.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Turning the message into a literal, physical act — text carved into skin for the AIGA poster, typography built from thousands of objects (coins, coffee cups, bananas), inflatable monkeys and room-scale typographic environments. The medium *is* the argument: the effort visible in the artifact validates the idea.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Closes the studio for a full year every seven years to experiment without client pressure — he says nearly everything produced in the following years traces back to sabbatical experiments. Trusts intuition and emotional honesty over trend; pursues beauty and optimism as serious design positions (Beauty, Now Is Better); willing to be slow and personal.

principle.title: Bank fallow time as a deliberate input
principle.explanation: Sagmeister's year-every-seven sabbatical isn't rest for its own sake — it's the upstream source of his best client work, because uncommitted experimentation produces the raw material that briefs later draw on. Originality is downstream of unscheduled, unpressured exploration.
principle.howToApply: Block recurring, protected time with no deliverable attached — a day a week, a week a quarter, or a longer break — and use it purely to experiment. Keep a diary of what you make and learn there, then pull from that well when real projects land instead of inventing under deadline.

entries.tagline: ~20+ equal owner-partners, no CEO, no house style — autonomy held together by shared equity and twice-yearly peer critique.
entries.summary: Pentagram is the world's largest independent design consultancy, structured as a true partnership of around two dozen designer-owners (Paula Scher, Michael Bierut, Harry Pearce, Natasha Jen, Eddie Opara, Marina Willer among them). Every partner owns an equal share of the firm, runs their own small team, keeps their own clients and P&L, and takes an equal cut of profit regardless of how much business they brought in. There is no CEO, no holding company, and no mandated house style — partners pool overhead and meet twice a year to openly critique each other's creative work and financials. The model is notable because it pairs near-total creative autonomy with genuine collective scrutiny, producing diverse, partner-authored work under one trusted name.
whatWorks: Equal ownership over hierarchy: every partner owns the same share and earns the same, so status comes from respect and craft rather than rank or sales.
Small, partner-led teams keep the maker close to the client — the person who wins the work also does and signs the work.
No enforced house style: a shared name can hold radically different voices if the bar for quality is shared instead of the aesthetic.
Twice-yearly open critique of both work AND money makes autonomy safe — freedom is balanced by mandatory, honest peer review.
Pooled overhead + self-funding (no outside shareholders) removes pressure for quarterly growth and protects creative independence.
Extremely selective, slow partner admission (existing partners must unanimously want you) keeps the culture and standard intact as it scales.

sections.name: How the studio is structured
sections.notes: ~20+ equal partners, each an owner of the whole firm. No CEO, CFO, board, or holding company — the partners collectively are the governance. Income is equal and profit is shared regardless of individual billings; offices in London, NY, SF, Berlin, Austin.

sections.name: How it takes on work
sections.notes: Each partner runs a small team (roughly a dozen designers) and keeps their own clients, projects, and P&L. Partners can collaborate across disciplines when a project needs it, but essentially operate as autonomous studios under one shared roof and brand.

sections.name: Signature method
sections.notes: No shared methodology, no visual house style, no client specialisation — diversity is deliberate. The unifying method is peer critique: at twice-yearly all-partner meetings (chaired on rotation) they dissect each other's creative work and compare financials openly.

sections.name: What holds it together
sections.notes: Equal equity, pooled overhead, and shared (unwritten) values — a love of design and wanting to leave a mark. Self-capitalised, so no external shareholders. New partners are admitted only through a long, near-unanimous vetting process, protecting the culture.

principle.title: Pair autonomy with mandatory peer critique
principle.explanation: Giving talented people full ownership of their work produces the best, most distinctive output — but autonomy without accountability drifts. Pentagram balances total creative freedom with a non-negotiable ritual of open, peer-to-peer scrutiny of both the work and the numbers, so quality stays high without a boss policing it.
principle.howToApply: In your own practice or teams, let each lead fully own their clients and output rather than enforcing one style or sign-off chain — then institute a recurring, candid review where peers critique each other's work and results in the open. Make the shared standard the quality bar and the values, not the aesthetic.

entries.tagline: The studio that answered austere Swiss modernism with drawing, wit, and the whole history of art as a sample library.
entries.summary: Push Pin Studios was founded in 1954 by Cooper Union friends Seymour Chwast, Milton Glaser, Edward Sorel, and Reynold Ruffins, who ran it as a flat, illustration-led collective rather than a hierarchical agency. Rejecting the 'numbing rigidity' of Swiss modernism and rote commercial realism, they mined past visual languages — Art Nouveau, Victoriana, Art Deco, comics, folk and woodcut traditions — and remixed them with humor and contemporary energy. They generated their own style and clients through self-published showcases, first the bi-monthly Push Pin Almanack and later the Push Pin Graphic, which doubled as promotion and creative laboratory. Their eclectic, drawing-first approach reshaped American illustration and earned a 1970 retrospective at the Louvre's Musée des Arts Décoratifs.
whatWorks: Run a self-published showcase (Almanack/Graphic) that doubles as promotion, sketchbook, and client magnet — make the marketing and the R&D the same artifact.
Treat art history as an open sample library: study a period style deeply, then remix it in your own hand rather than inventing from a blank page.
Keep the structure flat and 'unstructured' — no hierarchy, shared deadlines — so energy goes into creativity, not org charts.
Pair opposites on purpose: 'Milton would propose and Seymour would resist' — productive friction between dissimilar temperaments sharpens the work.
Lead with drawing and illustration, not layout grids — let imagery and wit carry the idea.
Reject the dominant orthodoxy deliberately: define your voice against what everyone else is doing (here, Swiss minimalism).

sections.name: How the studio began
sections.notes: Four Cooper Union students — Chwast, Glaser, Sorel, Ruffins — started it in 1954; Chwast and Sorel had already been mailing The Push Pin Almanack to ~1,500 art directors bi-monthly, a witty self-promotion that generated enough calls and assignments to keep them out of dead-end studio jobs.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Illustration-led, not modernist-grid-led. They consciously departed from the 'numbing rigidity of modernism' and sentimental commercial realism, instead reviving and reinterpreting historical styles (Victorian, Art Nouveau, Art Deco) and remixing sources from Renaissance painting to comic books and woodcuts.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Eclecticism plus wit plus the human hand: 'bulgy' three-dimensional interpretations of old styles, exaggerated forms, humorous juxtapositions, saturated color — every job filtered through drawing rather than photography or pure typography.

sections.name: What it changed
sections.notes: Pushed American graphic design from modernist orthodoxy toward playful visual pluralism across posters, album covers, and corporate work; influenced a generation of illustrators. A 1970 retrospective at the Louvre's Musée des Arts Décoratifs marked the first time graphic design entered that museum.

principle.title: Make your promotion your laboratory
principle.explanation: Push Pin's self-published Almanack and Graphic were simultaneously marketing, experimentation, and portfolio — so improving the work and winning the work were the same activity, and the studio's voice developed in public on its own terms instead of inside client briefs.
principle.howToApply: Start a small recurring self-published artifact (a zine, newsletter, or monthly experiment) where you try the styles and ideas you can't yet sell, then send it to the exact people you want as clients — let it be both your R&D and your business development.

entries.tagline: A world-renowned restaurant lets its photography and a long handwritten-feeling letter carry the brand, with type and chrome stepping almost entirely out of the way.
entries.summary: Noma's site reads like a printed culinary journal rather than a marketing page. A near-invisible nav and tiny wordmark cede the entire frame to honest, naturally-lit photography — architecture, ingredients, plated dishes, the team by a pool — while a long centered announcement letter set in a quiet serif slows the reader down. Everything sits on calm off-white with charcoal text, so the only color comes from the food and places themselves. The result feels confident and unhurried: the design's restraint is the luxury signal.
whatWorks: Tiny lowercase wordmark and a single thin row of nav links keep chrome under ~5% of the hero — the photograph owns the first screen
Full-bleed hero photo of the actual building (not a stock kitchen) establishes brand fidelity instantly; honest documentary imagery throughout
A long centered prose letter set in a measured serif at a narrow column width turns an announcement into an editorial reading moment
Alternating rhythm: full-bleed wide shot → tight two-up grid → centered text → full-bleed again, so the eye gets paced rather than fed a uniform card wall
Palette discipline — off-white ground, charcoal type, zero decorative color; the dishes and rooms supply all saturation
Generous vertical whitespace between every block signals premium pacing and lets each image breathe as its own page

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: Full-bleed photograph of the restaurant's building/courtyard, edge to edge; minuscule lowercase 'noma' wordmark top-left and a thin single-line nav. No headline overlay — the image is the statement.

sections.name: Two-up image grid
sections.notes: A tight pair of images (still-life of ingredients/vessels beside a warm-toned dish), no captions competing, letting texture and natural light do the talking.

sections.name: Letter / announcement
sections.notes: 'Copenhagen 2026' and a long centered letter in a refined serif at a narrow measure. Reads like correspondence; the restraint and column width make a text block feel intentional and intimate.

sections.name: Full-bleed group photo
sections.notes: Wide cinematic shot of the team gathered by a pool — humanizes the brand with a real, candid moment rather than a posed corporate portrait. Breaks up the reading with a breath.

sections.name: Closing feature + book
sections.notes: A featured dish/plant still-life paired with a short caption and a subtle link, plus a book/merch nod — soft commerce that stays in the editorial register.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Clean multi-column link grid (reservations, projects, news, careers, legal, social) under the small wordmark on the same off-white ground — quiet, organized, no heavy divider or dark slab.

principle.title: Let the photograph be the hero, not the headline
principle.explanation: When imagery is genuinely strong and on-brand, overlaying type or shrinking it into a card dilutes it. Noma proves a hero can carry meaning with a full-bleed honest photo and almost no chrome — the confidence reads as luxury.
principle.howToApply: On a hero or feature section with a strong real photo, drop the overlay headline and CTA stack; let the image go full-bleed with only a tiny wordmark + thin nav, and move the message into a quiet text block below where it can be read deliberately.

site.name: Burger King — Mobile geofencing / app-driven stunt

entries.tagline: Turned 14,000 competitor stores into Burger King ad space — buy a 1-cent Whopper only by standing inside a McDonald's.
entries.when: Launched December 4, 2018 in the US; ran nine days through December 12, 2018. Created by FCB New York with Braze. Won the Direct Grand Prix at Cannes Lions 2019.
entries.summary: The Whopper Detour weaponized location: the BK app offered a Whopper for one cent, but only when the user was within 600 feet of a McDonald's, then GPS-routed them to the nearest Burger King to collect it. The creative leans on a blurred Golden Arches gag that makes the troll legible in a single frame without naming the rival. It's a rare case where the media buy IS the joke — the offer mechanic and the punchline are the same thing, which is why it earned 3.5 billion impressions and 1.5M downloads off a near-zero paid spend.
whatWorks: Hook is conceptual, not visual fluff — the absurd 'go to McDonald's to get a Whopper' premise stops you cold
Blurred Golden Arches communicate the target instantly and legally, no logo or name needed
One-cent price is the headline; a concrete, almost-free number beats any adjective
CTA is the action itself — download, walk in, get routed out — no separate 'learn more' friction
Proof baked into the stunt: real-world geofencing made it feel like a live game, not an ad
Restraint — minimal copy, the mechanic carries the story so the creative stays clean

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The contradiction: the one place you can claim the deal is inside your enemy's store. Pattern-interrupt premise that demands a double-take.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'Order a Whopper for 1¢ at McDonald's.' Specific price + impossible location does all the lifting; tone is playful trolling, never mean.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: Blurred McDonald's arches + the penny Whopper. The blur is both a legal device and a wink — instantly readable as 'the other guys.'

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: No abstract ask. The app download and the GPS detour are the call to action — behavior is the conversion, measured in downloads and orders.

principle.title: Make the mechanic the message
principle.explanation: The strongest stunts collapse the offer, the joke, and the call to action into one inseparable move, so the campaign can't be paraphrased without doing it. That self-contained quality is what makes media earn itself.
principle.howToApply: Before adding explanatory copy or a separate CTA button, ask whether the core interaction already tells the whole story. If the user's action and the punchline are the same gesture, cut the surrounding scaffolding.

entries.tagline: Built the visual language of modern wayfinding by treating identity as a reproducible system, not a logo.
entries.summary: Otl Aicher co-founded the Ulm School of Design and led the design of the 1972 Munich Olympics, where he created the now-universal stick-figure pictogram system. He treated design as applied rationality: every mark derived from an explicit grid, rule set, and reason. His work proved that a brand is a system of constraints anyone can operate, not a single precious artifact.
whatWorks: Define the grid and construction rules FIRST, then derive every element from them — so the system, not your hand, guarantees consistency
Reduce each form to a fixed geometric vocabulary (here: a 0/45/90-degree angle kit) and forbid anything outside it
Design for the worst-case viewing condition (motion, distance, translation) rather than the ideal studio crop
Write the rationale down — Aicher justified every decision in argument, making the system teachable and defensible
Test legibility by stripping color and detail until only the essential silhouette carries the meaning
Build families, not one-offs: solve the structure once so the 200th icon costs almost nothing

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: Starts from the problem's logic and constraints, not a sketch. Defines the underlying grid, module, and rule system before drawing a single finished mark — the brief is reframed as 'what system will generate all required outputs.'

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Systematic derivation: establish a strict geometric framework (a mesh grid, a limited set of angles and proportions) and generate every individual element by applying those rules. Consistency is engineered into the construction, so hundreds of pieces stay coherent without ad-hoc styling.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: The Munich pictograms — human figures built only from a fixed kit of horizontals, verticals, and 45-degree diagonals on a common grid. Radical reduction to a shared geometric alphabet made every sport instantly readable and infinitely extensible across languages.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Good design is rational and explainable; ornament without reason is rejected. Clarity, reproducibility, and function over personal expression. If a choice can't be argued from the system's logic, it doesn't belong.

principle.title: Design the rules before the artifacts
principle.explanation: When you fix the grid, module, and constraints up front, the system itself enforces consistency and scale — you stop re-deciding the same things and every new piece inherits coherence for free.
principle.howToApply: For 35353, before styling individual screens or ads, lock a generative rule set: one grid, a fixed icon construction kit (allowed angles, stroke, corner radius), and a documented spacing scale. Then derive each ad size and page from those rules so the six ad formats and the site read as one family.

entries.tagline: An award-winning task manager whose marketing page is as calm and orderly as the app it sells — the design is a live demo of the product's promise.
entries.summary: Things presents a to-do app the way the app itself feels: white ground, dark text, generous breathing room, and not one decorative flourish competing for attention. The hero leads with a single confident claim and a tappable intro video poster, then the page steps through one idea at a time — 'Simply Powerful', a tidy four-up grid of every platform it runs on, and a wall of press and Apple Design Award proof — each block spaced so it reads as its own unhurried screen. There is nothing to wade through, which is exactly the experience the product is selling: clarity and order arriving 'within the hour.'
whatWorks: The page induces the feeling the product promises — calm, ordered, uncluttered — so the visitor experiences the value proposition before buying it
Hierarchy from size and weight on a neutral palette (white ground, dark text, color reserved for interactive elements), never from decoration
A four-up platform grid (Mac, iPhone & Watch, iPad, Vision Pro) with identical card structure communicates 'native everywhere you work' through repetition, not copy
Real app screenshots carry every feature claim — the product is the imagery, no abstract illustration filling space
Credibility stacked late and tastefully: Apple Design Award marks plus WIRED / MacStories / Wirecutter logos, each quote given vertical room so the proof never reads as a cluttered carousel
Restraint extends to the components — a single privacy-reassured newsletter field and a quiet multi-column footer, no heavy slab or color block to close

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: Centered logo and one line — 'the award-winning personal task manager' — over a large 'Watch Introduction Video' poster showing the real interface. One claim, one action, lots of air; the product UI is the visual.

sections.name: Simply Powerful
sections.notes: A single value-prop beat with an icon and a short promise (organize your tasks 'within the hour') plus a 'See features' link — accessibility over feature-listing, stated in one calm block.

sections.name: Platform grid
sections.notes: Four equal cards — Mac, iPhone & Watch, iPad, Vision Pro — each with a mockup, OS requirements, price, and App Store button. Equal visual weight makes the cross-device story scannable in one pass.

sections.name: What People Are Saying / Read All About It
sections.notes: Social-proof and press section: Apple Design Award graphics anchor credibility, then publication logos (WIRED, MacStories, Wirecutter) with highlighted quotes, each separated by generous spacing so acclaim reads as curated, not noisy.

sections.name: Newsletter
sections.notes: A minimal email field with an explicit privacy promise ('We'll only ever use your address to send you this newsletter') — trust through transparency, sitting quietly before the footer.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Six left-aligned link groups (Products, Support, News, Company) in restrained type with repeated social icons — comprehensive navigation without color or density.

principle.title: Make the page feel like using the product
principle.explanation: When a product sells a feeling — calm, focus, order, speed — the marketing site should induce that same feeling, so the visitor lives the value proposition instead of just reading it. Things proves clarity by being clear; a busy, decorated page would quietly contradict the promise of a tool that organizes your life.
principle.howToApply: Name the core feeling your product delivers, then audit the landing page against it: if the product promises calm but the page is loud, the page is lying. Pace one idea per screen, let real product UI carry the claims, and treat restraint as a demonstration of the value, not just a style.

entries.tagline: Anchor a global brand to a belief, not a product — and accept losing part of the audience to deepen loyalty in the rest.
entries.when: Launched September 5, 2018 ('Just Do It' 30th anniversary); Wieden+Kennedy Portland, produced via Park Pictures; won a 2019 Creative Arts Emmy (Outstanding Commercial) and Cannes Lions Grand Prix (Outdoor / Creative Effectiveness).
entries.summary: Nike marked 30 years of 'Just Do It' by handing the narration to Colin Kaepernick — the most polarizing athlete in America — and built a two-minute film around a single belief rather than any shoe. Cinematic, near-monochrome footage of athletes chasing improbable dreams resolves into Kaepernick's line, 'Believe in something. Even if it means sacrificing everything.' The ad deliberately risked alienating a slice of customers (#BoycottNike trended, products were burned) because the stance bonded Nike harder to the audience that shared the value — and the controversy itself became billions of dollars of earned distribution.
whatWorks: Leads with a belief, not a product — the shoes barely appear; the ad sells what Nike stands for
Casting IS the message: putting Kaepernick at the center makes the stance unmistakable and unfakeable
Reframes its own tagline — 'Don't ask if your dreams are crazy. Ask if they're crazy enough.' turns 'crazy' from insult into aspiration
Cinematic, near-monochrome film with calm voiceover gives a commercial the gravity of a documentary
Courage as strategy — willingly forfeiting part of the audience signals conviction and deepens loyalty in the target
Built to be argued about: the controversy was the media plan, generating billions of earned impressions off the paid spot

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: A close, quiet portrait and Kaepernick's voice opening the film — the most talked-about athlete in the country, which makes you stop and reckon with the stance before any sport appears.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'Believe in something. Even if it means sacrificing everything.' plus the reframed JDI line about dreams being 'crazy enough' — the ad argues for conviction itself, with the product as a silent sponsor.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: Near-monochrome, cinematic footage of athletes (LeBron, Serena, Odell Beckham Jr., Eliud Kipchoge) chasing improbable goals, narrated as one continuous belief — restraint and gravity over flashy product shots.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: Soft and brand-led — the swoosh and #JustDoIt. No hard ask; the film trusts the stance to drive search, debate, and allegiance rather than a click.

principle.title: Take a stand — sell the belief, not the feature
principle.explanation: A brand willing to risk part of its audience by standing for something earns far deeper loyalty from the audience that shares the value. Conviction is polarizing by design, and the polarization is what makes it spread — a safe, universal message persuades no one in particular.
principle.howToApply: When a brand has a genuine point of view, lead a campaign (or a hero section) with the belief and let the product recede. Pick a position you'd accept losing some customers over, embody it with a credible messenger, and frame the copy as a claim about what you stand for — not a list of features.

entries.tagline: 'Astonish me' — art direction as the relentless pursuit of the unprecedented, with the double-page spread treated as a single cinematic unit.
entries.summary: Alexey Brodovitch was the Russian-American art director who reinvented the modern magazine across 24 years at Harper's Bazaar (1934–1958), composing photography, type, and white space into spreads that read like film. His Design Laboratory — a teaching studio that trained Richard Avedon, Irving Penn, Diane Arbus, Robert Frank, and Joel Meyerowitz — pushed students to reject the familiar and discover their own eye. His method was physical and editorial: lay every image out at once, then design by selecting, cropping, and sequencing rather than perfecting a single frame, all under one demand to his collaborators — 'Astonish me.'
whatWorks: Design the spread, not the page — compose across the gutter as one balanced field of image, type, and space
Lay all the raw material out physically (photostats on the floor) and design by arranging and re-arranging at speed — composition as editing
Treat white space as an active element with a job, never as leftover margin
Crop boldly and off-center to inject movement, and let images bleed off the edge
Sequence pages cinematically — echo a pose or shape across spreads so the eye flows from page to page like a film
Set collaborators a high bar with minimal direction ('Astonish me') so they reach past their defaults toward the unprecedented

sections.name: How he begins
sections.notes: Starts with quick hand sketches of the layout, then works physically — photostats of every candidate image spread across the floor — editing by selecting and shuffling them at speed. He thinks in spreads and full sequences from the first move, never one isolated page.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: The double-page spread as a single cinematic composition. He balances photography, typography, and generous white space across the gutter, and paces the whole issue like a film — building rhythm, contrast, and momentum from page to page rather than designing each page on its own.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Bold, unexpected cropping and full-bleed images; activated white space; type set as dynamic, arresting form; and motifs echoed across pages for narrative flow. He pushed into the experimental — torn edges, isolated body parts, blur — to keep the eye surprised, and gave photographers like Avedon room to invent rather than prescriptive briefs.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: 'Astonish me' — if it has been seen before, it isn't good enough. He demanded the unprecedented from himself and his students, judged work by whether it broke from the familiar, and led the Design Laboratory to help each person 'discover his individuality and crystallize his taste' rather than copy a house style.

principle.title: Design the sequence, not the single screen
principle.explanation: Brodovitch never perfected one frame in isolation — he laid out all the material at once and composed the whole flow as a film, using white space as pacing and echoing motifs to carry the eye forward. A page (or a screen) judged alone can be beautiful yet break the rhythm of the experience around it.
principle.howToApply: On a scroll-based page or an ad sequence, lay every section or frame out together at small scale and design the flow first — where it breathes, where it hits, what repeats. Treat white space as deliberate pacing, crop hero imagery boldly, and hold each section to the 'astonish me' test: if it looks like the default, push it further.

entries.tagline: A data-journalism studio whose homepage turns its whole archive into a wall of vivid color blocks and bold serif headlines.
entries.summary: The Pudding publishes visual essays, and its homepage treats the index itself as a piece of design: a three-column grid where every story is a saturated solid-color tile topped by a tiny issue number and date, with a heavyweight serif headline below. Instead of stock photography it leans on flat color and hand-drawn sticker icons, so the page reads as confident, playful, and unmistakably its own. The restraint of black-on-white type against electric color makes a dense archive feel browsable rather than overwhelming.
whatWorks: Solid saturated color blocks stand in for thumbnails - no stock photos, so the grid never looks generic and loads instantly
Each card pairs a tiny mono issue-number + date tag with a big bold serif headline: catalog precision plus editorial voice
Hand-drawn sticker icons (search, faves, popular, envelope) carry all the personality, keeping the type system clean
Promo blocks (newsletter signup, merch) are dropped inline as grid cells, so they feel native instead of like banners
A thin black marquee bar at the very top advertises the newest interactive piece without stealing the layout
The serif logo is repeated big in the footer as a sign-off, bookending the page

sections.name: Top bar + header
sections.notes: A slim black strip runs a marquee plug for the latest game/story; below sits a centered script-serif 'The Pudding' wordmark flanked by sticker-style About / Subscribe / More links. Tiny, but it sets the playful-but-precise tone.

sections.name: Story grid
sections.notes: Three columns of equal tiles, each a flat vivid color (magenta, lime, yellow, coral, violet) carrying an issue number top-left and date top-right, then a bold serif headline and one-line dek beneath. Color is the only imagery, which is what makes the dense archive feel light.

sections.name: Inline promos
sections.notes: A newsletter capture and a merch block are slotted in as grid cells rather than full-width interruptions - same width and rhythm as the stories, so the ask reads as part of the catalog.

sections.name: Filter / 'Our Faves' row
sections.notes: A staff-picks blurb and a filter strip (Our Faves / Popular / Updating / Your Input / Video / Audio) let readers re-cut the same grid by interest, acknowledging different audiences without a second layout.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Donate and Subscribe stickers, the big serif logo repeated as a sign-off, and tidy link columns (Our Team, Pitch a Story, social). The repeated logo closes the page like the cover of a magazine.

principle.title: Let flat color do the work of imagery in a content grid
principle.explanation: Thumbnails are the hardest part of an index to keep consistent - sourcing, cropping, and quality vary, and mismatched images make a grid look cheap. Swapping photos for a system of saturated solid-color tiles guarantees visual consistency, loads instantly, and reads as a deliberate brand choice rather than a placeholder.
principle.howToApply: On a blog index, resource library, or case-study grid with no strong hero images, assign each card a flat brand-palette color block plus a bold headline instead of hunting for stock photos. Keep the type system quiet and let the color rhythm carry the page.

site.name: Oatly - YouTube / Super Bowl film ("Wow, No Cow")

entries.tagline: An ad so deliberately bad - the CEO singing off-key alone in a field - that its awkwardness is the hook.
entries.when: Originated in Sweden 2014; re-run as Oatly's U.S. Super Bowl LV debut, Feb 2021
entries.summary: Oatly's CEO Toni Petersson stands alone in an oat field with a cheap keyboard and sings, badly, 'It's like milk, but made for humans.' There is no product demo, no gloss, no aspirational lifestyle - the spot is intentionally amateur, and that anti-advertising posture is exactly what makes it stick and get shared. It closes on the hand-drawn, textured 'The Original OATLY!' wordmark over the same field, so all the polish the film withholds is concentrated into one confident brand frame.
whatWorks: The hook is anti-craft: a flat, off-key jingle reads as honest and unguarded, disarming ad-skepticism
One human, one line of copy, one location - radical reduction makes the message impossible to miss
The tagline IS the lyric, so the claim and the earworm are the same six words
Self-aware enough to be a meme: people share it precisely because it 'shouldn't' have aired
All visual polish is saved for the end card - the textured hand-drawn wordmark does the brand work the film refuses to

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: A man alone in a field at a keyboard, singing slightly off-key. The wrongness of it stops the scroll - it doesn't look or sound like an ad, so you keep watching to figure out what it is.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'It's like milk, but made for humans.' The entire proposition delivered as a sung lyric - product truth and hook fused into one repeatable line.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: Naturalistic, un-color-graded field and grey sky; no product beauty shots. The plainness is the point - it signals 'we're not performing for you.'

sections.name: CTA / sign-off
sections.notes: Cut to the hand-drawn, ink-textured 'The Original OATLY!' lockup over the field. No 'buy now' - just the brand stated with total confidence, the one polished asset in the whole spot.

principle.title: Withhold polish on purpose to read as honest
principle.explanation: Audiences are fluent in advertising and discount anything that looks slick. A deliberately rough, low-production execution flips that instinct: it signals the brand isn't trying to manipulate, which earns attention and sharing. The discipline is concentrating your craft into one or two moments (here, the end wordmark) rather than spreading gloss everywhere.
principle.howToApply: When a brand voice can carry honesty, let one element be deliberately plain - a hand-set headline, an un-retouched photo, a single human talking - and reserve the polished, designed moment for the sign-off so the brand still lands sharp.

entries.tagline: The information architect who treats typography as a system of rules you can actually follow.
entries.summary: Spiekermann designed FF Meta, FF Unit, ITC Officina and corporate faces for Deutsche Bahn, Audi and others, and co-founded MetaDesign and FontShop. He calls himself an information architect rather than a stylist: his work is about making complex information legible at speed under real constraints. His book 'Stop Stealing Sheep & Find Out How Type Works' turned a lifetime of craft into plain-language rules, and that instinct - codify it so others can repeat it - runs through everything he does.
whatWorks: Start from the constraint, not the look: FF Meta began as 'legible, neutral, space-saving' for a postal service, and the form followed those rules
Define the antithesis - he designed Meta as 'the complete antithesis of Helvetica,' using an enemy to sharpen every decision
Solve at the smallest scale: he obsesses over letter-spacing and counters because that's where legibility is won or lost
Write the rules down so a whole team (or reader) can apply them consistently - design as transferable system, not personal taste
Judge type by performance - does it read fast and clearly at the size and distance it'll actually be used - before beauty

sections.name: How he begins
sections.notes: With the brief's hard constraints and context of use: who reads this, at what size, how fast, in what conditions. The Bundespost spec (legible, neutral, space-saving, unmistakable characters) literally generated FF Meta's narrow letters and open counters.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Treat typography as information architecture: structure and hierarchy first, so the reader's eye is routed before any styling. He thinks in systems - families, weights, and spacing rules that hold across an entire identity, not one-off layouts.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Micro-tuning at the character level - strokes thin enough to keep counters open but thick enough to stay legible, spacing engineered so letters never run together. Tiny adjustments that compound into readable text at small sizes.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Design is communication in service of a function, not decoration ('form follows function, but it doesn't have to be boring'). Define what you're against, make rules you can follow, and codify the craft so it's repeatable - hence 'Stop Stealing Sheep.'

principle.title: Turn your craft into rules you can hand to someone else
principle.explanation: Spiekermann's edge isn't a signature style; it's that he reduces hard-won judgment into explicit, followable rules - about spacing, hierarchy, and legibility - so quality survives across a team and across applications. Codified craft scales; taste alone doesn't.
principle.howToApply: After solving a design problem well, write down the rule you discovered (e.g. 'body text 16px+ with 1.5 line-height,' 'never more than two type sizes per card') and add it to a project's design tokens or a short style note, so the next page and the next person get it right by default.

entries.title: Fix the tracking & hierarchy of a display title
entries.skill: Optical letter-spacing at large sizes, measure, and baseline hierarchy — the invisible adjustments that separate amateur from set type.
entries.brief: Take a loosely-set hero headline and tighten it into a confident, hierarchy-clear lockup.
steps: Drop the starter snippet into CodePen (or rebuild the same lockup in Figma with the provided text).
Tighten the H1 tracking — large display type needs NEGATIVE letter-spacing; aim for about -0.02 to -0.03em so the letters lock together.
Set the measure: keep the headline to 2 lines, constrain its width (~16ch) so it breaks well, and add text-wrap: balance.
Build hierarchy: eyebrow = small, uppercase, +0.12em tracking, muted; subhead ≈ half the H1 size, regular weight, ink-soft color.
Fix vertical rhythm: tighten H1 line-height (~1.03), give the subhead air (~1.5), and set deliberate gaps between eyebrow → H1 → subhead.

assets.label: Starter HTML + CSS (fix this)
assets.code:
design journal

Build a working library of what good looks like.

A daily study of design done well — one principle at a time.

entries.successLooksLike: The headline reads as one tight unit (no gappy letters), breaks across two balanced lines, and the eye lands eyebrow → headline → subhead without effort.
entries.stretch: Do it twice — once in CSS, once in Figma — and compare how each tool handles optical tracking and line breaks.

entries.title: Set body copy in two columns — kill widows, orphans & rivers
entries.skill: Column setup, measure (line length), justification/hyphenation, and spotting the rendering flaws that make text look unprofessional.
entries.brief: Flow the provided copy into two clean columns and remove the typographic gremlins.
steps: Make a text frame and split it into 2 columns with a ~24px gutter (Figma: text-box columns; InDesign: Object > Text Frame Options; CSS: column-count:2).
Set the measure to 45–75 characters per line — adjust size/width until each line holds roughly 9–12 words.
Choose alignment deliberately: left-aligned (ragged) is safest; if you justify, turn ON hyphenation so you don't open rivers.
Hunt the gremlins: no lone word on a paragraph's last line (widow), no single line stranded at a column top/bottom (orphan), no vertical 'rivers' of gaps.
Tidy: pick paragraph spacing OR a first-line indent (never both), and balance both columns so they bottom-align.

assets.label: Body copy
assets.value:
Good design is mostly decisions made twice — once on instinct, once on purpose. The first pass finds the idea; the second pass defends it, trimming everything that does not earn its place. Restraint is not the absence of effort but its concentration.

A page is a sequence of attention. The eye lands somewhere first, then follows the path you built for it: a headline, a figure, a line of type that explains the rest. When that path is clear, the work feels effortless. When it is not, the reader does the designer's job — and resents it.

Treat structure as the message. A grid, a measure, a consistent rhythm: these are not constraints on expression, they are the medium of it.

assets.label: Specs
assets.value: 2 columns · 24px gutter · measure 45–75 ch · 16–18px body · line-height 1.5 · left-aligned, or justified + hyphenated

entries.successLooksLike: Both columns read evenly, no widows/orphans/rivers, a comfortable measure, and the block looks like a magazine spread rather than a wall of text.
entries.stretch: Rebuild the same block in CSS with `columns: 2`, `hyphens: auto`, and `text-wrap: pretty`, and see how close you get to your Figma version.

entries.tagline: A serious open-source analytics platform drawn as a friendly engineering sketchbook — hand-drawn hedgehogs instead of the usual dark-tech gradients.
entries.summary: PostHog sells a deeply technical product (product analytics, session replay, feature flags, experiments) but rejects the entire visual genre of B2B dev tools. Instead of somber dark gradients and 3D abstractions, it runs a warm, creamy, textbook-illustration world populated by custom hand-drawn hedgehogs — a proprietary illustration system maintained by full-time in-house illustrators. The result is instantly recognizable, impossible to clone with stock assets, and pitched perfectly at engineers who distrust polished sales language. The copy is just as deliberate: self-aware, transparent, and funny.
whatWorks: A proprietary hand-drawn illustration system (the hedgehogs) replaces stock/3D dark-tech tropes — instant, un-clonable differentiation in a sea of identical SaaS sites
The hero leads with personality (a mascot scene), not a product screenshot — brand before feature
Creamy off-white, sketchbook palette instead of the genre's default dark gradients — warmth reads as contrast and confidence
Radical pricing transparency: real per-unit costs shown ($0.00005/event) and a genuinely generous free tier as a trust signal
Anti-corporate, self-aware copy ('Yes they actually use us...', 'Shameless CTA') earns credibility with skeptical engineers
One platform shown as a modular stack of distinct, individually-illustrated tools — generous whitespace keeps the density scannable

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: Minimal nav (product, pricing, docs, community) plus a 'Get started – free' CTA, anchored by a large hand-drawn hedgehog scene. Personality and a clear free entry point land before any feature claim.

sections.name: Social proof
sections.notes: Customer logos with a 'Shuffle companies' interaction and a parenthetical aside ('Yes they actually use us...'). The honest, joking tone defuses the usual logo-wall skepticism.

sections.name: Product stack / tools
sections.notes: The suite (analytics, session replay, feature flags, experiments, surveys) shown as a modular grid of cards, many with their own little illustration. Communicates 'one platform, many real tools' without a feature dump.

sections.name: Pricing
sections.notes: Usage-based pricing with actual unit economics on display and a large free tier. Showing the real numbers (rather than 'Contact sales') is itself the trust move for an engineering buyer.

sections.name: Why PostHog / culture
sections.notes: Links straight to the public company handbook, fast shipping cadence, and engineer-run support — turning operational transparency into brand differentiation.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: A dense mega-footer (docs, community, company) with playful easter eggs and self-deprecating CTA copy ('Shameless CTA') — even the chrome stays on-voice.

principle.title: Draw your own world — make custom illustration the moat
principle.explanation: In a category where every competitor reaches for the same dark gradients, 3D blobs, and stock photography, a consistent proprietary illustration system is the one asset rivals can't copy. It carries personality, tone, and recognition simultaneously, and it compounds: every new drawing reinforces the same unmistakable world.
principle.howToApply: When a brand sits in a visually homogeneous category, commission (or build) a small, consistent illustration vocabulary — a character, a drawing style, a palette — and use it everywhere instead of stock. Even a handful of custom spots in a recognizable hand will out-distinguish a site of polished but generic 3D renders.

entries.tagline: Heavy-metal horror branding wrapped around… canned mountain water.
entries.summary: Liquid Death inverts the entire visual language of bottled water. Where the category is serene blues, mountains, dewdrops and wellness, Liquid Death wears a gothic-metal logo, a skull, and a 'murder your thirst' tallboy can that looks like a craft beer or energy drink. The packaging itself is the primary medium — it markets on the shelf and in every hand — amplified by darkly comic social videos. It turned the most boring product imaginable into an entertainment brand people tattoo on themselves, with a real sustainability argument (aluminum, 'Death to Plastic') riding inside the joke.
whatWorks: Category inversion: adopt the codes of the OPPOSITE category (energy drink / metal / horror) so a water can becomes unmissable
The product IS the ad — the tallboy can does the marketing on the shelf and in every photo, no media buy required
Tone as a moat: dark, self-aware comedy competitors can't copy without looking desperate
A real value smuggled inside the gag — 'Death to Plastic' / recycled aluminum gives the irreverence substance
It behaves like an entertainment IP, not a CPG: merch and a 'Country Club' turn buyers into a tribe
One locked system — gothic blackletter-ish type, skull mark, black + bone-white — makes every touchpoint instantly its own

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: Cognitive dissonance stops the scroll: a murder-metal 'death' can that turns out to be just water. It doesn't compute, so you look twice.

sections.name: Message
sections.notes: 'Murder Your Thirst' / 'Don't be scared, it's just water.' Threat-comedy reframes ordinary hydration as rebellion and an in-joke.

sections.name: Visual
sections.notes: A tallboy aluminum can in black and bone-white with a dripping horror logo and skull — reads as craft beer or energy drink, never as Evian.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: Low-friction and identity-driven: buy the can, join the Country Club. The purchase is sold as joining a culture, not consuming a beverage.

principle.title: Wear the opposite category's clothes
principle.explanation: The fastest way to stand out in a visually homogeneous category is to borrow the aesthetic codes of a different, more exciting one — then anchor the stunt to one true value so it's subversion with substance, not just shock.
principle.howToApply: When designing in a sea of sameness (another calm fintech, another serene wellness brand), list the category's visual clichés, then deliberately dress the brand in an adjacent category's energy. Pin it to one genuine truth (a real value, a real benefit) so the contrarian look has a reason to exist beyond noise.

entries.tagline: The Netherlands' first big multidisciplinary studio — design as rational teamwork on a grid.
entries.summary: Founded in 1963 by Wim Crouwel, Friso Kramer, Benno Wissing and the brothers Paul and Dick Schwarz, Total Design (TD) was the first large-scale multidisciplinary design consultancy in the Netherlands — created so Dutch institutions wouldn't have to hand major work to foreign agencies. Under Crouwel's creative direction it treated design as rational, systematic teamwork: grids, neutral sans typography, and a documented house method. Across decades of Stedelijk Museum graphics, wayfinding and identities, TD proved a studio could scale craft through system rather than through a single star designer.
whatWorks: Start every project from a grid and a tight type palette — system first, expression second
Staff multidisciplinary by design: graphic + industrial + spatial designers under one roof so big 'total' commissions can actually ship
Pair the makers with business partners (the Schwarz brothers ran organization and finance) so designers spend their time designing
Divide labor on a shared system — many hands stay coherent because the grid and rules hold the work together, not one person's taste
Treat neutral typography as a stance: suppress designer ego and let the system and content speak
Use a long client relationship (the Stedelijk Museum) as a living lab to refine one system over decades

sections.name: How it's structured
sections.notes: A founding partnership that mixed disciplines on purpose — graphic (Crouwel), industrial (Kramer), spatial (Wissing) — plus the Schwarz brothers running organization and finance, so the design side could focus on design while the business held.

sections.name: How it takes on work
sections.notes: Built for large, complex 'total' commissions — identities, wayfinding, exhibition graphics — that a solo designer can't hold. Clients get one studio responsible for the whole coherent system.

sections.name: Signature method
sections.notes: Rigorous grid systems plus neutral sans typography (Akzidenz/Helvetica-era). The modular grid is the unit of work, letting many designers contribute consistently; Crouwel's New Alphabet pushed systematic, constructed type to its logical extreme.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Rational over expressive: choices you can defend from the system's logic. The system — not the individual — is the author, and consistency held across a long relationship beats one-off cleverness.

principle.title: Build a system many hands can execute as one
principle.explanation: Total Design's breakthrough was organizational as much as visual: by encoding decisions into a documented grid and a neutral type palette, a multidisciplinary team could split a huge commission and still ship one coherent voice. The system absorbs ego and scales craft past what any single designer can touch.
principle.howToApply: On any project bigger than yourself, invest first in the shared scaffold — grid, tokens, type scale, and a short written house style — then parcel out the work. Coherence comes from everyone building against the same system, not from one person having to touch every screen.

entries.title: Trace a curve in the fewest anchor points
entries.skill: Drawing clean Bézier curves with minimal anchor points and tangent-aligned handles
entries.brief: Rebuild a simple curved shape with the Pen tool using as few anchor points as possible — placing each one only at a curvature extreme — so the outline is smooth and editable.
steps: Paste the starter SVG below into a 600×600 artboard (or open it) and lock it on its own layer at 40% opacity — it's an over-anchored, lumpy leaf shape. This is your reference.
On a new layer, redraw the same leaf with the Pen tool, placing anchors ONLY at the top, bottom, left and right extremes of the curve (the north/south/east/west rule) — never mid-curve.
Drag each handle along the tangent of the curve; keep handle length around one-third of the segment so the curve stays smooth without overshooting.
Close the path, then switch to the Direct Selection tool and nudge handles until your outline kisses the reference with no kinks at 400% zoom.
Count your anchors. Try to beat your first pass by deleting one point and re-smoothing the neighbours.

assets.label: Goal
assets.value: Match the reference leaf using 4–6 anchor points total (the starter uses ~10).

assets.label: Starter SVG (rebuild this with fewer points)
Rule of thumb
assets.value: Anchor points belong at extremes (N/S/E/W) and at sharp corners — nowhere else. If a point sits mid-curve, you have one too many.

entries.successLooksLike: A smooth leaf outline matching the reference, built from ~4–6 anchors placed at curve extremes, with no visible bumps or kinks when you zoom to 400%.
entries.stretch: Hand-write the same shape as a raw SVG <path> 'd' string using cubic Bézier (C) commands, matching your Illustrator anchor positions — then diff it against the starter to see how much shorter clean curves are.

entries.tagline: A tech company's book imprint that treats its website like fine print design — covers as heroes, type and whitespace doing all the work.
entries.summary: Stripe Press publishes 'ideas for progress' as beautifully produced books, and the site mirrors that craft. It is almost entirely typographic — no stock photography, no gradients — with the physical book covers (often foil/letterpress, presented as animated 'living covers') acting as the only imagery. A restrained grayscale palette with a single hyperlink blue, generous whitespace, and one consistent per-book template make a content-dense catalog feel like a quiet, premium reading room.
whatWorks: Let the product's own artifact be the imagery: animated 'living' book covers are the only visuals, so the page inherits the books' craft
Near-monochrome palette plus one accent (hyperlink blue) — restraint reads as intellectual confidence
Typographic hierarchy alone (weight, size, space) carries the page; no decorative chrome
A single repeating per-book module (title → author → cover → description → praise → buy) builds rhythm and cuts cognitive load
Generous gutters make a dense catalog feel curated rather than crowded
A deliberate burst of color in the documentary section breaks the monochrome to signal a different content type

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: 'Stripe Press — Ideas for progress' set large in type with no hero image; positions the imprint as content-forward and confident from the first screen.

sections.name: Book grid
sections.notes: A multi-column grid of titles, authors, and cover thumbnails. The covers are the visual anchors in an otherwise text-led layout.

sections.name: Individual book treatment
sections.notes: A repeating module per book — living cover, description, multi-retailer buy buttons, author bio, praise blockquotes, and a downloadable zine PDF. Identical structure every time.

sections.name: Documentary / film section
sections.notes: Colorful abstract stills ('We Are As Gods') deliberately break the monochrome to mark a different medium without breaking the overall system.

sections.name: Newsletter & footer
sections.notes: A quiet signup and a minimal footer; the chrome stays subordinate to the content throughout.

principle.title: Design one module and repeat it without flourish
principle.explanation: A catalog feels premium and calm when every item uses the identical template — same order, same spacing, same blockquote style — so the eye learns the pattern once and the content (not the layout) becomes the variable. Bespoke per-item layouts read as noise; strict uniformity reads as editorial confidence.
principle.howToApply: For any list or catalog (products, case studies, books), define one item template down to spacing and quote styling, then apply it identically to every entry. Resist the urge to make each item special; let repetition create rhythm and let the content provide the variety.

entries.tagline: Coca-Cola replaced its own iconic logo with 150 first names — turning every bottle into a personal invitation.
entries.when: Launched 2011 (Australia); rolled out to 120+ countries in 2013–2014
entries.summary: Coca-Cola did something almost heretical: it gave up the most valuable logo position in the world and printed people's first names in its place. 'Share a Coke with [Name]' turned a mass product into a personal, giftable object and a social-media engine — people hunted for their names, photographed them, and tagged friends. It reversed a sales decline in Australia (250M+ named bottles sold in a nation of ~23M) and scaled to over 120 countries.
whatWorks: Sacrifice sacred brand real estate (the logo) to make the customer the hero of the pack
Personalization at scale: hundreds of names plus inclusive fallbacks ('Mate', 'BFF') so almost everyone is covered
Build in a treasure hunt — finding your name is a small reward that drives store visits and repeat looks
Engineer shareability: a named bottle is a pre-made social post and a tagging prompt
Keep the rest of the system intact (Spencerian script, Coke red) so it still reads instantly as Coca-Cola
Tie digital to physical: free name-printing kiosks made the campaign an event, not just packaging

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: You see your own name where the Coca-Cola logo should be — a double-take that personalizes one of the most familiar brands on earth.

sections.name: Message
sections.notes: 'Share a Coke with [Name]' reframes buying a soda as a small act of giving it to a specific person.

sections.name: Visual
sections.notes: The familiar red bottle and flowing script, but the wordmark swapped for a first name — all the equity, new personal meaning.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: Find your name and share it — implicit, social, and physical (kiosks), never a hard sell.

principle.title: Give your logo's spot to the customer
principle.explanation: The most personal thing you can put on a product is the customer themselves. Trading even sacred brand real estate for the user's name (or photo, or data) converts a mass object into something they own and broadcast — so the customer becomes your distribution channel.
principle.howToApply: Find the one place users expect to see YOUR branding and consider handing it to them — a name, their stats, their content — on a generated asset that is easy and rewarding to share. Keep the rest of the brand system fixed so it stays unmistakably you.

entries.tagline: Turned a whole city into a navigable identity — Mexico '68's pulsing logo and pictogram wayfinding.
entries.summary: Lance Wyman is a father of modern wayfinding. For the 1968 Mexico City Olympics he immersed himself in Mexican culture — pre-Hispanic art, Huichol yarn paintings, local sign-painting, plus 1960s Op Art — and from that built a total identity: the radiating 'Mexico 68' logo, a pictogram system, color-coded transit maps, and supergraphics that turned the city into a legible space. His method — research a place deeply, then derive a systematic visual language from it — helped define environmental graphic design (later the Mexico City Metro, Washington Metro, the National Zoo).
whatWorks: Begin with immersive field research: days in museums and streets absorbing the local visual culture before drawing anything
Derive the system from the place — let indigenous and vernacular forms (Huichol lines, Op Art) generate the logo and grammar, not an imported style
Design for everyone: pictograms and color-coding that work across languages and literacy levels
Make one motif generative: the radiating-line logo seeds banners, uniforms, signage, and the whole environment
Think in environments, not artifacts — balloons, supergraphics, and maps turn the city itself into the medium
Treat legibility at distance and speed (transit, crowds) as a hard design constraint

sections.name: How he begins
sections.notes: Buys a one-way ticket and does immersive, on-the-ground research — over a week in the Museum of Anthropology and the streets — absorbing local forms before he sketches anything.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Extracts a visual DNA from the place (pre-Hispanic geometry, Huichol yarn-painting lines, Op Art) and builds a coherent system from it rather than applying a house style.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: A single radiating-line motif that scales from logo to typography to citywide supergraphics, plus language-independent pictograms and color-coded transit routes.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Judge by navigation for real people in real space: does it help a stranger find their way at speed, across languages and literacy levels?

principle.title: Mine the place for the system
principle.explanation: The strongest identities aren't imported styles — they're derived from deep research into the specific context (culture, vernacular, environment). Wyman's Mexico '68 worked because its visual grammar grew out of Mexico itself, then was applied systematically across every touchpoint.
principle.howToApply: Before designing an identity or product world, do field research into its specific context — the place, the users' vernacular, the existing signage — pull out a few generative forms, and build the whole system from those rather than from a trend. Then test it where it will actually be used (at distance, across languages).

entries.tagline: The world's most-copied public service site — plain language, task-first IA, and a rigorously documented, accessible design system.
entries.summary: GOV.UK consolidated hundreds of UK government sites into one, and it has become the global reference for public-sector and systems-driven design. It leads with search and the most-used services, organizes everything by user task ('Money and tax', not 'Treasury'), writes in plain language, and is built on the open GOV.UK Design System — accessible components, the legible GDS Transport-derived typeface, and strict content standards. It is the anti-flashy benchmark: design as clarity, accessibility, and trust at national scale.
whatWorks: Organize by user task and life event, not by internal org structure
Plain-language labels everywhere ('Check your State Pension forecast', not departmental jargon)
Search-first hero: discovery as the primary action for an enormous service catalog
A documented, open, accessible design system (components + content style) so thousands of teams stay consistent
Accessibility as a baseline: skip links, high contrast, legible type, tested with assistive technology
Ruthless restraint: near-zero decoration so content and tasks dominate

sections.name: Header / nav
sections.notes: A simple menu grouped by plain-language categories, mirrored in the footer so the structure is consistent top and bottom.

sections.name: Search hero
sections.notes: A prominent search box positions findability first — the right primary action for a catalog of thousands of services.

sections.name: Popular services
sections.notes: The most-used tasks (HMRC account, Universal Credit) surfaced immediately, driven by real usage data rather than politics.

sections.name: Services & information grid
sections.notes: Card modules grouped by life event and task (Benefits, Housing, Citizenship) — categorized by user need, not government department.

sections.name: Government activity
sections.notes: Departmental news and content kept clearly separate from the services, so 'doing a task' and 'reading about government' never blur.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Support links, an explicit accessibility statement, and the same nav structure repeated for consistency.

principle.title: Organize by the user's task, not your org chart
principle.explanation: People come to do a thing, not to learn your internal structure. GOV.UK's biggest move is labeling and grouping everything by user task in plain language — which only works because it sits on a documented system that keeps thousands of pages consistent and accessible.
principle.howToApply: Audit your nav and labels: are they named after departments and features, or after what users are trying to do? Rewrite them as user tasks in plain language, group by life events / jobs-to-be-done, and back it with a small component and content system so consistency survives scale.

entries.tagline: Cropped the golden arches into directional signs — the logo itself becomes the wayfinding.
entries.summary: Cossette's 'Follow the Arches' sliced McDonald's logo into fragments that double as directional arrows — the left arch as a left turn, the right as a right, the top as a U-turn — so a billboard literally points you to the nearest restaurant using nothing but pieces of the brand mark. Minimal color, minimal text, no food photography; it works because the arches are so deeply recognizable that even a fragment reads instantly. It won Cannes, Clio and D&AD honors.
whatWorks: Exploit brand recognition: a logo so iconic that a cropped fragment still reads as the brand
Make the logo do a job — turn identity into functional wayfinding information
Radical reduction: minimal color, almost no copy; the idea needs nothing else
Language-independent: arrows work for any driver, in any language
A repeatable system: the same logic generates left / right / U-turn / next-exit variants
The confidence to crop — trusting the audience to complete the shape is the whole gag

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: An abstract yellow fragment on a billboard that your brain instantly completes as McDonald's — recognition lands before you have read a single word.

sections.name: Message
sections.notes: Directional copy ('next exit', 'just missed us') — the brand mark itself tells you where to go.

sections.name: Visual
sections.notes: A single cropped section of the golden arches functioning as an arrow, on a minimal ground, with no food photography.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: Follow it — the sign is the call to action, leading you to the nearest restaurant.

principle.title: Crop your logo until it does a job
principle.explanation: A truly iconic mark survives fragmentation — and that surplus recognition is an asset you can spend. McDonald's turned brand equity into functional information (directions) by cropping the logo, proving the audience will complete a shape they already know.
principle.howToApply: If your brand mark is well-known, test how little of it still reads (a corner, a color, a curve) and use that fragment functionally — as navigation, a loader, a cropped hero, a favicon-as-system. The more you can remove and still be recognized, the stronger and more flexible the identity.

entries.tagline: Gave the computer a face — the original Macintosh icons, fonts, and the friendly pixel.
entries.summary: Susan Kare designed the original Macintosh icons, typefaces and visual language (1983–86): the Happy Mac, the trash can, the watch cursor, the Command (⌘) symbol, and bitmap fonts like Chicago and Geneva. Working at 16×16 and 32×32 pixels, she made computing feel approachable and human. Her method — sketch the metaphor on graph paper as if doing needlepoint, then place each pixel deliberately — turned a hostile machine into something warm and effectively founded icon design as a discipline.
whatWorks: Start from the metaphor, not the pixels: name the idea ('boot', 'find', 'danger') before drawing
Sketch on graph paper first — one square per pixel — treating icons like needlepoint or mosaics
Embrace the constraint: the tiny grid forces clarity and a single readable idea per icon
Borrow from craft traditions (counted-thread embroidery, mosaic, pointillism) for pixel thinking
Design for instant recognition and warmth — make the symbol friendly, not technical
Build a coherent family (icons + fonts + cursors) so the whole system feels of one hand

sections.name: How she begins
sections.notes: With the concept or command to be represented — sketchbook pages titled 'boot', 'jump', 'debug' — choosing a metaphor a stranger will read instantly.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Blocks a 16×16 or 32×32 square on graph paper and fills pixels by hand, exactly like a needlepoint or knitting pattern, before moving to the on-screen icon editor.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Maximal meaning from minimal pixels — the Happy Mac, trash can, watch cursor — plus hand-built bitmap fonts; warmth deliberately engineered into a tiny grid.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Is it instantly recognizable and friendly? Does it reduce to one clear idea at actual size? Craft and metaphor over technical accuracy.

principle.title: Solve the metaphor before the pixels
principle.explanation: Kare's icons endure because she nailed the idea — the right human metaphor — first, then executed it with craft inside a brutal constraint. The tiny grid clarified rather than limited; it forced one legible idea per symbol.
principle.howToApply: When designing any icon, symbol, or small UI element, first decide the metaphor in words and confirm it is universally readable; then design at the smallest real size you will ship, placing every pixel or point deliberately. Let the constraint force a single clear idea.

entries.tagline: Sells transformation, not rooms — full-bleed photography, near-silent copy, and extreme restraint as the luxury signal.
entries.summary: Aman's site is a master class in luxury restraint. It lets enormous, architectural, full-bleed photography of its resorts carry almost the entire experience, with spare, evocative copy ('Moments of Transformation') and a strictly neutral white/gray/black palette so nothing competes with the imagery. Properties are organized geographically and presented as equal-weight modular cards; interactions are quiet (collapsing menus, gentle hovers) rather than flashy. The generous whitespace and refusal to clutter are themselves the message: exclusivity reads as calm.
whatWorks: Let full-bleed, architectural photography do the selling and keep copy to a few evocative words
Keep the UI palette strictly neutral so the only color on the page comes from the photography
Use generous whitespace as a luxury signal — emptiness reads as exclusivity, density reads as discount
Present destinations and experiences as equal-weight modular cards so a large brand stays scannable
Favor quiet, purposeful motion (menu collapse, subtle hovers) over animation spectacle
Manage expectations transparently with status badges ('Opening 2027', 'Reopening Soon') without breaking the calm
Keep one consistent 'Reserve' CTA placement so booking is always a single move away

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: A single full-bleed property image (e.g. Amanvari, two chairs facing the sea) with minimal copy; the photograph communicates luxury before a word is read.

sections.name: Destinations
sections.notes: Properties grouped geographically (Americas & Caribbean, EMEA, Asia) as a scannable directory, some flagged 'Coming Soon' or 'Reopening Soon'.

sections.name: Experiences
sections.notes: Four equal-width cards (art, wellness, private villas, escapes) — modular consistency carrying content variety.

sections.name: Brand ecosystem
sections.notes: Aman at Sea, Shop, Interiors and Gift Cards given equal visual weight, extending the brand narrative beyond accommodation.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Comprehensive regional destination lists and global social links (including WeChat/Red) signalling international reach.

principle.title: Make photography the only source of color
