sections.notes: A gold 'Subscribe to The Times.' link names the goal, then a solid black 'SEE MY OPTIONS' button softens the commitment into a browse.

principle.title: Sell the conviction, then the click
principle.explanation: A consideration‑stage ad converts on belief, not features. One values‑led sentence in the brand's own typeface does more than a spec list, and a soft CTA verb makes the first click cheap.
principle.howToApply: For a consideration ad or hero, lead with a single belief‑statement set in your brand's real display face, drop the photo if the words are strong enough, and pair a naming link with a low‑friction button ('see my options', not 'buy now').

entries.tagline: 'Mr Gridnik' — turned the grid into a total operating system and treated technical constraints as the design itself.
entries.summary: Crouwel co‑founded Total Design and gave Dutch modernism its most systematic voice — the Stedelijk Museum's posters and catalogues (1964–93), the experimental New Alphabet, and the Gridnik typeface. His work reduced design to grids, fixed type and rational rules, and it turned the limits of a medium into visible logic rather than hiding them.
whatWorks: Design the system (grid, type, format, rules) first; treat every finished piece as a predictable output of it.
Turn the constraint into the form — New Alphabet (1967) was built only from horizontal and vertical strokes to honour early screen limits.
Solve recurring problems by building a typeface or module you can reuse, creating economies of decision‑making.
Test a concept in its real application (he reworked the Rabobank wordmark after seeing it in a 3D light‑box) instead of judging it flat on paper.
Keep radical experiments separate from client deliverables so ideas can mature without risking the brief.
Choose functional clarity over personal expression — legibility and order are the point, not the designer's signature.

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: Define a modular grid and a strict rule set before any layout — the system is the first deliverable; individual pieces come out of it.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Reduce to essentials, standardise (typeface, grid, format), and let the system generate consistency across posters, catalogues and wayfinding for decades.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Constraint‑driven letterforms — New Alphabet honours cathode‑ray limits; Gridnik is drawn on a fixed modular grid; the medium's limit becomes the aesthetic.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Be 'technologically honest': don't disguise the medium's constraints, make them the readable logic. Function beats self‑expression; clarity is the judge.

principle.title: Build the system before the page
principle.explanation: Crouwel's coherence came from designing rules — grid, type scale, format, constraints — first, so every artifact is a predictable output rather than a fresh invention. It scales and stays unified, and honouring a constraint can become the form.
principle.howToApply: Before designing screens, define the grid, type scale and a short list of constraints (spacing steps, allowed weights); compose only within them. When a medium imposes a limit, make it the visible logic instead of fighting it.

entries.tagline: A travel magazine's entire archive rendered as one hushed typographic index — whitespace as the main character.
entries.summary: Cereal's site is its whole back catalogue (888 entries) rebuilt as a single, radically calm index: a spaced‑out wordmark, a plain‑text filter rail (Categories / Search Terms / Year), and a sparse masonry of article titles with tiny id numbers, punctuated by the occasional large photograph. Imagery loads on scroll, so the page reads as a quiet list of names first and pictures second. (In a static full‑page capture many images appear as placeholders — it's genuinely best experienced live.)
whatWorks: Archive as a text‑first index, not a wall of thumbnails — titles and ids carry navigation, so 888 entries feel calm instead of overwhelming.
A warm cream ground and tiny serif captions set a hushed, print‑like register that signals editorial confidence.
Enormous margins and air; the few large duotone/still images that do appear feel earned rather than decorative.
The filter rail (Categories / Search Terms / Year 2024—2017) is powerful archive navigation kept as plain text with no UI chrome.
A consistent numbering system ([005‑04], [018‑12]) runs quietly underneath as an ordering device.

sections.name: Masthead & nav
sections.notes: Letter‑spaced 'C E R E A L' top‑left with minimal 'Search / Information' links; nothing competes with the content.

sections.name: Filter rail
sections.notes: Right‑aligned Categories / Search Terms / Year (a plain column of years) — archive power tools rendered as quiet text.

sections.name: Results index
sections.notes: 'Results: 888 · Sort by: Chronologically', then a sparse grid of article titles with id numbers, punctuated by a few large photographs (a still‑life, a white staircase, warm interiors).

sections.name: Image on demand
sections.notes: Photographs load as you scroll rather than all at once, so the page reads as a list of names first — in a static capture many show as placeholders; the site is best seen live.

sections.name: Load more & footer
sections.notes: A quiet 'Load more', then a centred newsletter signup — no busy mega‑footer to break the calm.

principle.title: Let whitespace do the curating
principle.explanation: Cereal makes a huge archive feel considered by leading with typography and air and revealing images only on demand. Restraint reads as editorial confidence, and each photograph that does surface feels chosen rather than dumped.
principle.howToApply: For content‑heavy indexes (blogs, galleries, docs, case‑study libraries), try a text‑first list with generous margins and lazy/hover‑loaded imagery instead of a thumbnail grid; the page feels curated and loads light.

entries.tagline: A tall unit split into 'aspiration on top, action below' — one boot hero, one script word, one loud button.
entries.summary: Merrell uses the height of a 300×600 to tell a two‑beat story: the top half is a full‑bleed forest photograph with the boots as the single focal point, and the bottom half is a clean white action zone. Script product name over letter‑spaced benefit copy, then one saturated‑orange CTA and the wordmark. The emotive image and the calm message never fight.
whatWorks: Clear vertical zoning — an image‑led 'why' on top, a quiet 'do this' below; the tall format is used as a reading axis, not just more space.
One focal point (boots on a stump) in a real environment sells a place, not just a product.
The white lower third gives type room to breathe against a busy photograph — no text‑on‑image legibility gamble.
Two type registers do two jobs: a warm script 'Ontario' (the model / feeling) over spaced sans 'TRAIL‑READY PERFORMANCE' (the function).
One reserved accent — saturated orange 'SHOP NOW' is the only loud colour, so the eye lands on the CTA; the wordmark anchors brand recall.

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: A full‑bleed forest photograph with boots perched on a stump — stops the scroll with mood and place before any words.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: Lower white panel: script 'Ontario' over letter‑spaced 'TRAIL‑READY PERFORMANCE' — emotive and functional in two registers.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: Single focal point and a warm natural palette; the clean lower third keeps the type legible and calm against the busy image.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A saturated orange 'SHOP NOW' — the only loud colour in the unit — with the Merrell wordmark beneath for recall.

principle.title: Zone a tall ad: aspiration up top, action below
principle.explanation: A 300×600 has room for a two‑beat story. Merrell gives the top to an emotive full‑bleed image and the bottom to a calm white action zone, so the unit sells the feeling then routes the click without the two competing.
principle.howToApply: In any tall/portrait creative or hero, split it into an image‑led 'why' zone and a quiet, high‑contrast 'do this' zone, and reserve one saturated accent colour solely for the CTA.

entries.tagline: Pentagram co‑founder who made wit a working method — ideas by 'looking sideways', reduced to one clever mark.
entries.summary: Fletcher was Britain's most influential postwar graphic designer, co‑founding Fletcher/Forbes/Gill and then Pentagram, and creating marks like the V&A monogram and the Reuters dot logo. His method was lateral thinking and relentless collecting — he treated design as 'a way of life', mined a lifelong scrapbook for oblique connections, and reduced each idea to a single witty move.
whatWorks: Treat design as a way of seeing, not a task — sketch and observe constantly so input never stops.
Collect relentlessly (notebooks, scrapbooks, cuttings) and use the hoard as raw material for unexpected connections.
Solve by lateral association and visual pun rather than illustrating the brief literally.
Reduce to one idea, cleanly executed — the V&A logo turning the ampersand's counter into the 'A'; Reuters built from 84 dots.
Use wit as a test of quality: if a mark rewards a second look, the idea is real; if it's merely decorative, it isn't finished.
Let big ideas mature over years (18 years on 'The Art of Looking Sideways').

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: Constant collecting and sketching — a brief starts by ransacking a lifelong scrapbook of images and observations for an oblique connection.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Lateral thinking: find the unexpected association or visual pun that reframes the problem instead of depicting it head‑on.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Radical reduction to a single witty mark — the V&A ampersand‑as‑A monogram, the 84‑dot Reuters logo; one idea, executed cleanly.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Ask: is there a real idea, and does it reward a second look? Wit is evidence of intelligence, not garnish; give big ideas years to mature.

principle.title: Look sideways, then cut to one idea
principle.explanation: Fletcher's best marks come from an oblique association nobody else spotted, reduced until a single clever move carries the whole thing. Wit isn't decoration — it's proof the idea is real.
principle.howToApply: When stuck, stop illustrating the brief literally: list adjacent objects, words and metaphors, hunt for a surprising overlap, then reduce to the one element that makes people look twice. Keep a running scrapbook so you always have raw material.

entries.tagline: A product catalog designed like an instrument: full-bleed hardware photography on a stark white grid, wordless custom icons, and near-zero marketing chrome.
entries.summary: Teenage engineering treats its site the way it treats its products — reductive, precise, and confident enough to leave most of the page empty. The homepage is a wordmark plus four wordless icon clusters (products, store, latest, finder); the product index is a vertical stack of enormous, edge-to-edge hardware photographs, each device shot flat and razor-sharp on white so the object itself carries the page. Type is one tight custom geometric sans, colour is ~95% black-on-white with a single signal-orange accent, and marketing copy is almost absent: a product name, a model number, a one-line descriptor, done. It is Swiss-industrial restraint applied to e-commerce, where the grid and the photography do all the talking.
whatWorks: One signal colour: the palette is essentially black on white, and a single saturated orange marks only what is new or actionable (the 'EP SERIES UPDATE' badge, the version numbers) so attention has exactly one place to land.

Product-as-hero photography: each device is shot flat, centred and edge-to-edge at huge scale on pure white, sharp enough to read every button label — the object is the layout, not a supporting image.
Wordless custom iconography: the top nav uses bespoke pictograms instead of a text menu, which reads as brand confidence and rewards familiarity.
Radical copy restraint: a product gets a name, a model number and a single descriptor line ('128 MB SAMPLER COMPOSER') with no feature bullets competing with the image.
Whitespace as pacing: generous empty bands separate each product block so the long scroll feels like turning pages in a catalogue, never a crowded grid.
One tight technical typeface ties wordmark, nav, labels and product names into a single engineered voice.

sections.name: Hero / index nav
sections.notes: The page opens not with a banner but with the brand as an index: the 'teenage engineering' wordmark, four wordless icon clusters (products / store / latest / finder), the hexagonal TO logo, and a small block of Japanese mission text. It frames the site as a tool you navigate rather than a pitch you read.

sections.name: EP SERIES update block
sections.notes: A compact left-aligned module announcing firmware updates — 'EP SERIES / UPDATE' with a solid orange bar, then a tiny table of models and version numbers in orange. It is the one place colour and 'news' concentrate, sitting quietly above the product river.

sections.name: Product hero rows
sections.notes: The catalogue is a vertical stack of full-bleed product photographs (RIDDIM, K.O. II, field-system devices), each shot flat and gigantic on white with only a name, model and one-line descriptor. Scale plus sharpness make the hardware the entire visual argument.

sections.name: Product detail / macro photography
sections.notes: Scrolling into a product shows its face at near 1:1 — every knob, key and silkscreen label legible — so the photography doubles as spec sheet and seduction; no rendered mockups, just the honest object.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: A restrained multi-column footer: Stockholm address, ©2026, newsletter signup, policies, retailers and the free-shipping threshold — utilitarian, quiet, and set in the same single-typeface system.

principle.title: Let the product be the entire layout, and ration everything else
principle.explanation: When you sell something that is itself well designed, oversized honest photography of the real object out-argues any amount of marketing copy or decoration. Stripping the chrome — menus become icons, features become a single line, colour shrinks to one accent — doesn't weaken the page; it signals that the product can carry it, which reads as confidence and quality.
principle.howToApply: On a product or portfolio page, shoot or render the real artifact flat and sharp, place it edge-to-edge at large scale on a plain ground, and give it only a name plus one descriptor line. Restrict colour to a single signal accent reserved for what is new or clickable, and set everything in one tight typeface. When a section tempts you to add explanatory copy, first try making the image bigger instead.

entries.tagline: Acid-green headline on pure black, one ghost CTA, and the logo lockup as the only other element — a B2B banner that reads in half a second.
entries.summary: This is B2B display distilled to four elements: a black field, a two-line acid-green headline, a bordered 'Read the eBook' ghost button, and the DocuSign eSignature lockup. There is no product shot and no illustration — the extreme contrast of neon green on black does the stopping, a number ('6') promises a scannable payoff, and the offer is honest about being an eBook, a low-commitment consideration-stage ask. It is the anti-clutter banner: everything not essential to one click has been removed.
whatWorks: Maximum contrast as the hook: near-fluorescent green type on pure black pops in a crowded feed or GDN slot with no imagery at all.
A number in the headline: '6 overlooked strategies' promises a finite, scannable payoff and primes the reader for a listicle eBook.
Benefit-led copy: 'that deliver rapid growth' names the outcome, not the format — the value is stated before the ask.
Ghost CTA sized right: 'Read the eBook' is a bordered outline button, present but not shouting, and its verb matches the low-commitment offer (read, not buy).
Brand where it belongs: the DocuSign eSignature lockup sits bottom-right, clearly present but subordinate to the message — attribution without stealing the focal point.
One focal point: no competing photo, badge or price; the eye travels headline → button → logo in a single pass.

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: Fluorescent green on pure black. With no image at all, the raw contrast is the scroll-stopper; the banner looks like a highlighter glowing on a dark screen.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: '6 overlooked strategies that deliver rapid growth.' A number for scannability, 'overlooked' for curiosity and mild FOMO, and the benefit ('rapid growth') stated plainly — all in two tight lines.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: There is no hero image; the typography IS the visual. Trusting type and colour to carry the unit keeps file size tiny and the message unmissable at banner scale.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A white-outlined 'Read the eBook' ghost button under the headline. The outline style reads as a button without competing with the neon headline, and the verb honestly matches a low-friction gated-content offer.

principle.title: Trust type + contrast when the offer is an idea, not a product
principle.explanation: For a consideration-stage gated asset (an eBook, a guide) there is no product to photograph, so a hero image would just be decoration. A high-contrast type-only banner loads fast, stops the scroll on colour alone, and lets a numbered, benefit-led headline do the persuading — with the CTA verb matched honestly to the small ask.
principle.howToApply: For content-offer banners, drop the stock photo. Set a numbered, benefit-first headline in the brand's boldest colour on a plain dark ground, add one ghost CTA whose verb matches the commitment ('read the guide', not 'get started'), and place the logo lockup small in a corner. Test it at the smallest size — if the headline still reads, it works.

entries.tagline: The typographer who codified modernist asymmetry in 'Die neue Typographie', then systematised Penguin's paperbacks with a single sheet of composition rules.
entries.summary: Jan Tschichold trained as a calligrapher, which gave him a craftsman's respect for letterforms and, unusually, a pragmatic streak: he built with stock typefaces and commercial paper rather than bespoke everything. In 1928 his book 'Die neue Typographie' codified modernist practice — sans-serif, asymmetry, function over ornament — into rules that working printers could actually follow. Then he did the opposite of dogma: he later renounced his own hardline modernism as too rigid, returned to classical typefaces, and at Penguin Books (1947–49) wrote the four-page 'Penguin Composition Rules' that standardised hundreds of titles while still letting each book breathe. His genius was turning taste into a transmissible system.
whatWorks: Write the rules down: he distilled his method into short, printable manuals (Die neue Typographie, the Penguin Composition Rules) so a whole team of printers could execute his standard without him in the room.

Systematise the many, personalise the one: a shared skeleton (grid, type sizes, spacing) across hundreds of books, but each cover and title page tuned to that book's character — consistency and individuality at once.
Build with stock, not bespoke: he chose commercial typefaces and standard paper stocks deliberately, so the system stayed reproducible and affordable rather than a one-off luxury.
Let function set the layout: asymmetry, type size and whitespace were chosen to serve information hierarchy and reading, never as decoration.
Be willing to renounce your own dogma: he publicly reversed his hardline modernism when he judged it too rigid, choosing the right tool over ideological consistency.
Sweat the micro-typography — spacing, punctuation and detail rules — because that is where he located quality.

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: He starts from the text and the constraints, not a blank aesthetic. Calligraphy training meant he read the content and the reading experience first, then asked what standardised materials (a stock face, a standard paper size) could serve it economically.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Codify the method into explicit, written rules. Rather than art-directing each piece by feel, he wrote manuals and composition rules that encode the decisions, so the standard becomes transmissible and repeatable across a team and hundreds of artifacts.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Systematic micro-typography and grid discipline: precise rules for type sizes, line spacing, indentation, punctuation and title-page layout — the small, near-invisible craft decisions applied consistently — plus, in his modernist phase, functional asymmetry driven purely by hierarchy.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Function and legibility over ornament; the nature of each work dictates its final look within the system. And, crucially, ideology is subordinate to results — he abandoned rules, even his own famous ones, when he decided they no longer served the reader.

principle.title: Turn your taste into written rules others can execute
principle.explanation: A design standard only scales if it lives outside your head. Tschichold's lasting impact came not from single beautiful pages but from short, explicit rule-sets — grids, type sizes, spacing, composition rules — that let printers and teams reproduce his quality across hundreds of artifacts, while a shared skeleton still left room to tailor each individual piece.
principle.howToApply: For any multi-artifact project (a site, a component library, a set of ads, a book series) write a one- or two-page 'composition rules' doc: the grid, the type scale, spacing rhythm, colour usage and the do/don'ts — concrete enough that someone else could produce an on-brand piece without you. Then allow deliberate per-piece variation on top of that fixed base, and hold the rules loosely, revising them when they stop serving the reader.

entries.tagline: A fintech homepage that makes banking feel calm and premium by pairing crisp product UI with restrained, abstract illustration and a lot of quiet space.
entries.summary: Mercury sells 'radically different banking' to startups, and the page earns the claim through composure rather than noise: near-white grounds, one refined sans, and a rhythm of full-width feature blocks that each pair a real product screenshot with a single benefit line. Abstract geometric illustrations (overlapping circles, ripples, upward graphs) carry the softer 'safety and control' ideas where a screenshot would be too literal, and hard proof — 300K+ entrepreneurs, 1-in-3 startups, $20B+ monthly volume, 4.9 App Store — lands as a clean stat row before the close. The result reads trustworthy and modern without a single loud gradient.
whatWorks: Product UI as the hero image: virtual cards, invoicing, search — real interface screenshots do the persuading instead of stock photography
Abstract illustration reserved for the intangibles (security, visibility, control) so every visual either shows the product or stands for a feeling — never decoration for its own sake
A four-number stat row (300K+, 1-in-3, $20B+, 4.9) turns positioning into evidence in one glance
Testimonials from recognizable founders (Linear, Gainful) borrow credibility from names the audience already respects
Generous vertical rhythm — one idea per screenful — keeps a feature-dense product from feeling cluttered
Accent color spent almost entirely on CTAs; the palette is otherwise white/gray so the buttons are the only thing that shouts

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: 'Radically different banking' set large over an animated product-dashboard frame, with an email field + 'Open account' inline. The CTA is a form, not just a button — one step closer to signup from the fold.

sections.name: Four-pillar value grid
sections.notes: Banking, cards/expenses, payments/invoicing, accounting in a clean 4-column row — the whole product surface mapped before any deep-dive, so visitors self-select which feature to care about.

sections.name: Founder testimonial carousel
sections.notes: Portraits + quotes from known startup founders on a light ground; social proof placed high, right after the value grid, while interest is fresh.

sections.name: Feature deep-dives
sections.notes: Alternating image/text blocks with real UI — virtual cards, bill automation, invoicing, receipt reconciliation. Alternating white/gray grounds create rhythm without borders.

sections.name: Stat / trust row
sections.notes: 300K+ · 1-in-3 · $20B+ · 4.9 in oversized numerals — the evidence beat that converts 'nice product' into 'serious, widely-trusted bank'.

sections.name: Security & FDIC section
sections.notes: 20x FDIC coverage, fraud protection, spend controls, each with a small data-viz illustration — de-risks the decision right before the close.

sections.name: Dual final CTA + dense footer
sections.notes: Side-by-side 'business' and 'personal' offers, then a charcoal multi-column footer with FDIC/partner-bank disclosure — compact, organized, and appropriately serious for a financial product.

principle.title: Split your visuals in two: screenshots for what it does, abstraction for how it feels.
principle.explanation: Feature-heavy products get muddy when every section tries to illustrate everything. Mercury shows the literal product with real UI and reserves abstract illustration only for intangibles (safety, visibility, control) — so no visual is mere decoration and the page stays legible.
principle.howToApply: Audit a product page's images: each should either be a real interface screenshot (a concrete capability) or an abstract mark standing for a feeling. Cut anything that is neither, and keep the accent color almost entirely on the CTAs so buttons stay the loudest thing on a quiet page.

site.name: Grammarly — mobile display unit (~258×512 portrait GDN)

entries.tagline: One flat illustration, a four-word promise, and a single green button — a mobile display unit that says exactly one thing.
entries.summary: This is Grammarly's mobile GDN unit reduced to essentials: green logo lockup at the top, a light-grey flat illustration in the middle (a figure walking past a speech bubble, a pen, and a growing sprout — writing + growth), then 'Instant Grammar Checker' with a one-line clarifier and a rounded green 'Try Now' pill at the bottom. Nothing competes for attention; the eye travels logo → image → headline → button in a single vertical sweep. It works because it commits to one benefit and one action and trusts white space to do the rest.
whatWorks: One idea only: 'Instant Grammar Checker' — no feature list, no secondary offer, so the unit is legible at a glance and at thumbnail size
Strict vertical hierarchy (logo → illustration → headline → CTA) matches how a thumb scans a portrait ad
Brand green used only twice — the logo mark and the CTA pill — so the button reads as the one thing to touch
Flat, lightweight line illustration keeps file size tiny (<15KB) for fast load, and stays on-brand without a photo
Sub-headline does one job — clarify the benefit ('Correct all grammar errors and enhance your writing') — then gets out of the way

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The instantly recognizable green Grammarly mark anchors the top; brand recognition is the scroll-stopper here rather than a shock visual.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'Instant Grammar Checker' in bold near-black — a literal, benefit-first promise, no cleverness, readable in under a second.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: A muted flat illustration (walking figure, speech bubble, pen, sprout) suggests writing and self-improvement without a literal screenshot — soft, friendly, low-contrast so the headline and button stay dominant.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A rounded, high-saturation green 'Try Now' pill floated in white space at the bottom — the only saturated element, unmissable and thumb-sized.

principle.title: In a small unit, say one thing and color only the button.
principle.explanation: A portrait mobile ad has room for exactly one message. Grammarly spends its whole hierarchy on a single benefit and reserves its brand color for just the logo and the CTA, so the action is never in doubt even at thumbnail scale.
principle.howToApply: When building a display/social unit, write the one benefit first, then subtract everything that isn't the logo, that benefit, or the button. Keep the creative near-monochrome and let the CTA be the only saturated element.

entries.when: Founded 2008 · Brooklyn & San Francisco, USA · led by Brian Collins
entries.tagline: The independent studio that reframed branding as designing an optimistic future clients 'can't help but want.'
entries.summary: COLLINS is one of the most awarded independent brand-experience studios of the last two decades, behind transformations for Spotify, Mailchimp, Dropbox, Twitch, Robinhood, Instacart and more. Its operating model treats design as 'a benevolent agent of change' — not styling a logo, but building a compelling thesis about a client's future and then making artifacts real enough that the future feels inevitable. What holds it together is a culture hired for 'contribution, not fit' and a relentless optimism Brian Collins calls letting 'form follow the fantastic.'
whatWorks: Start from a thesis about the future, not a moodboard: define the world the brand should create, then design backward into logo/system
Frame the work as a 'chain reaction' — one bold idea that unlocks business value, a new market, and internal culture at once
Sell inevitability: make the proposed future so compelling that saying no feels like opting out of progress
Prototype the feeling early — build vivid artifacts (films, environments, motion) so stakeholders experience the idea instead of debating a deck
Hire for 'cultural contribution, not cultural fit' to keep the studio's range wide and its ideas un-formulaic
Optimism as method: 'form follows the fantastic' — lead with wonder, then engineer the craft to justify it

sections.name: How it's structured
sections.notes: A deliberately independent, mid-size studio across Brooklyn and San Francisco — big enough for ambitious transformations, small enough to stay author-driven and avoid holding-company dilution. Hybrid/flexible working; a headquarters sized for culture, not headcount.

sections.name: How it takes on work
sections.notes: Engages clients at inflection points — moments of growth, merger, or reinvention — as a transformation partner, not a vendor. Pairs creative ambition with commercial logic so a bold idea also has a business case.

sections.name: Signature method
sections.notes: The 'chain reaction': craft a thesis about the client's future, then design the identity, environments, motion and story that make that future tangible. Design 'a future people can't help but want' rather than predicting one.

sections.name: What holds it together
sections.notes: A stated philosophy ('design is not what we make, it's what we make possible'), Brian Collins's 101 design rules, hiring for contribution over fit, and an insistence on optimism — the shared belief that a better version is designable.

principle.title: Design the future first, the logo second.
principle.explanation: COLLINS wins by arguing for a compelling future state and building artifacts that make it feel inevitable; the marks and systems follow from that thesis. It reframes the deliverable from 'a nicer identity' to 'a change worth believing in,' which is far harder to say no to.
principle.howToApply: Before touching type or color on a brand project, write one paragraph: what world does this brand help create, and why would people want it? Prototype that feeling early (a short film, a hero moment, a motion study) so the client buys the future — then let the visual system serve it.

entries.title: Fit a headline to a portrait display ad with CSS clamp()
entries.skill: Building a fluid, format-locked ad unit where type scales to the box instead of overflowing it
entries.brief: Recreate a Grammarly-style 300×600 portrait display unit as a single responsive CSS box. The headline must always fit on two lines and the CTA must stay pinned at the bottom, whether the unit renders at full size or scaled down to a 160-wide thumbnail.
steps: Build one .ad container fixed to a 1:2 aspect ratio (aspect-ratio: 1 / 2) with max-width 300px, so the unit keeps its format at any scale.
Lay the interior out as a 3-row grid: logo (top), illustration/placeholder (center, flex-fill), and a headline+CTA block (bottom).
Size the headline with clamp() so it scales with the box — e.g. font-size: clamp(15px, 7cqw, 24px) using container query units — and cap it to two lines with -webkit-line-clamp.
Give the CTA a full-color pill and reserve the brand color for ONLY the logo mark and the button; keep everything else near-mono.
Shrink the container to 160px wide and confirm the headline still fits two lines and the button stays pinned — fix with clamp() bounds, not media queries.

assets.label: Sub-headline
assets.value: Correct every mistake and sharpen your writing.

assets.label: Starter code (fix this — the headline overflows when scaled)

entries.successLooksLike: At 300px and at 160px the headline holds to two lines, never clips or overflows, and the green CTA pill stays glued to the bottom edge — all from clamp() + container units, zero media queries.
entries.stretch: Add a subtle 150ms transform:scale(1.03) hover on the CTA and a fade-up of the headline using a single @keyframes, keeping the unit under 20KB of CSS.

entries.tagline: A tasks-notes-and-plans app site that proves 'everything in one app' by letting a single running product demo carry the whole page.
entries.summary: Superlist sells a combined tasks + notes + plans app, and the page's whole job is to make 'one app for everything' feel true rather than claimed. It does that with a calm, near-monochrome layout that pushes the colorful product UI forward, a single declarative hero headline, and a reframing of AI as relief ('let AI do it for you') instead of a feature checkbox. Real App Store and Play reviews carry the proof.
whatWorks: Centered hero with one declarative headline ('Tasks, notes, and plans. Finally in one app.') and a single 'Sign up for free' CTA - no competing actions.
The hero image is the real product UI, not a stock photo, so the claim is demonstrated on sight.
The AI block leads with the pain ('stop spending time organizing') and frames the feature as relief, not a spec.
Three named use-case lanes (Plan your day / Stay on top of work / Keep home in sync) let different personas self-select on one page.
Modular feature cards keep dense functionality scannable - bold feature name over one light descriptor line.
Social proof is real store reviews from both iOS and Play, quoting varied user types for credibility.

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: Centered single-sentence headline + one primary CTA over a real product screenshot; generous whitespace so the app UI is the color.

sections.name: AI features
sections.notes: Reframes automation as relief ('let AI do it for you') - voice-to-tasks, meeting-note summaries - leading with the pain it removes.

sections.name: Use-case lanes
sections.notes: Three named tracks (Plan your day / Stay on top of work / Keep home in sync) so work and home personas self-select without separate pages.

sections.name: Feature cards
sections.notes: A modular grid of ~seven capability cards; bold feature name + one light line each keeps a broad feature set digestible.

sections.name: Social proof
sections.notes: Testimonials pulled straight from the iOS App Store and Google Play, varied by user type and platform for breadth of credibility.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Platform downloads (macOS / Web / iOS / Android) grouped with company, legal and social - download-first, matching a cross-device app.

principle.title: Let one running product demo be the spine of the page.
principle.explanation: When the differentiator is 'everything in one place,' fragmented feature shots quietly contradict the pitch. A single, continuous product thread proves the cohesion the copy is claiming.
principle.howToApply: On a product page, thread one persistent app mock - same workspace, same data - through hero, features and use cases so every section advances the same demo instead of resetting to a fresh unrelated screenshot.

entries.tagline: A dark cinematic square where four recognizable Marvel heroes and two logos do the selling - the copy is almost incidental.
entries.summary: This 300x250 leans entirely on owned IP: a movie-poster wall of Captain America, Black Widow, Thor and Iron Man over a dark blue gradient. The written pitch is a single line - 'The streaming home of Marvel' - with the Marvel logo doing the work of a sentence, and one blue CTA pill closing it. At thumbnail size, recognition sells faster than any headline could.
whatWorks: Leads with recognizable IP: four beloved Marvel heroes signal the value instantly, no reading required.
Dark blue cinematic gradient makes the characters and white type glow - a movie-poster feel at 300x250.
Copy is one line; a brand lockup ('The streaming home of' + Marvel logo) replaces an explanatory sentence.
A single saturated blue 'Start Free Trial' pill is the only clickable-colored element.
Clean top-to-bottom hierarchy: faces then brand then offer then action.

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: A wall of four famous Marvel heroes stops the scroll on recognition alone - the unit's whole stopping power is borrowed equity.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'The streaming home of Marvel' - a logo lockup instead of a written pitch; the offer is simply a free trial.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: Dark blue movie-poster collage; the Disney+ wordmark is centered as the anchor between the characters and the button.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: One high-contrast blue rounded pill, 'Start Free Trial,' the only saturated element against the dark ground.

principle.title: When you own the IP, let recognition be the creative.
principle.explanation: Recognizable faces, characters and logos are processed faster than any headline. In a 300x250 there's no room to explain, so lead with equity you already have and let it carry the stop.
principle.howToApply: If a brand has recognizable assets (characters, a famous product, a known partner logo), lead the small unit with them and cut the explanatory copy to a single lockup plus one CTA.

entries.tagline: The Bauhaus master who turned typography into a rational system - dropping the capital letter and treating type, photo and space as one integrated design.
entries.summary: Bayer ran the Bauhaus printing and advertising workshop and pushed design toward integrated, systematic thinking. His 'universal' geometric sans (1925) eliminated capitals on the logic that a single-case alphabet is faster to set and read, and he fused typography, photography and later architecture and exhibition design into one continuous practice. His value today is method, not nostalgia: question the convention, then build a reusable system.
whatWorks: Question a convention by asking what it's for - he dropped uppercase because one alphabet is more efficient to set and read.
Design the system, not the artifact - the 'universal' type was built to run across every Bauhaus publication, not one poster.
Integrate disciplines - he treated type, photography and (later) architecture and exhibitions as one design problem.
Use photomontage to make an argument, not to decorate - image and type composed as a single message.
Reduce letterforms to geometric essentials (circle + line) so the system stays consistent and reproducible.

sections.name: How he begins
sections.notes: From the communication problem and its constraints, then questions the inherited rules around it - why two alphabets, why serifs, why this grid.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Builds a reusable system: one geometric, single-case alphabet plus a consistent style and grid applied across all print, so every artifact stays coherent.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: The 'universal' geometric sans constructed from circles and straight lines, all-lowercase; and photomontage that integrates image and type into one composition.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Form serves function; efficiency and legibility over ornament; design solves problems across media rather than decorating a single surface.

principle.title: Interrogate the convention before you decorate the page.
principle.explanation: Bayer's strongest moves came from asking what an inherited rule is actually for and rebuilding from function - which yields reusable systems, not one-offs.
principle.howToApply: On any brand system, list the conventions you're inheriting (two type cases, a serif, a default grid) and, for each, ask 'what job does this do?'; keep only what earns its place and design the rest as a reusable rule.

entries.tagline: A cookware store that sells with food, not spec sheets - lifestyle photography and set-savings math carry a huge catalog.
entries.summary: Our Place is a DTC cookware brand, and its page converts on appetite: styled food and kitchen photography does the persuading while a muted beige/white/black UI stays out of the way. Product cards make value literal with set-savings callouts, strikethrough pricing and color-variant swatches, and trust messaging (100-day trial, free returns, 100,000+ 5-star reviews) is repeated right where the buy decision happens.
whatWorks: Muted beige/white/black palette so styled food and the colorful pan finishes are the only real color on the page.
The hero puts motion in a still - vegetables tossing mid-air in the wok - so cookware reads as alive and aspirational.
Every product card surfaces color variants as swatches, making finish choice part of the browse.
Value is concrete: set-savings callouts and strikethrough pricing on each card instead of a vague 'sale.'
Trust messaging (100-day trial, free returns, free shipping over $135, 100,000+ 5-star reviews) is repeated near the decision, not buried in the footer.
A category mega-menu with featured products keeps a large catalog low-friction to navigate.

sections.name: Header & mega-nav
sections.notes: White header, dark logo; category mega-menus (Cookware, Bakeware, Appliances, Tableware) each surface featured products and set savings, easing discovery across a big range.

sections.name: Hero carousel
sections.notes: Rotating hero anchored by a back-in-stock product (Carbon Steel Wok) with food in motion, a bold headline and one 'Shop Now' CTA; scarcity + a chef collab add pull.

sections.name: Promo banners
sections.notes: Collection features (Titanium set, 100-day trial) built from high-quality food and product photography rather than copy blocks.

sections.name: Bestsellers grid
sections.notes: Four-up cards with hover states, BESTSELLER badges, set-savings and strikethrough pricing; variant finishes shown as swatches on the card.

sections.name: Category tiles
sections.notes: Cropped photography tiles with category names only - minimal text, image-led routing into the main departments.

sections.name: Trust signals & reviews
sections.notes: Repeated 100-day trial / free returns / free shipping band, plus '100,000+ 5-star reviews' with a portrait-photo testimonial carousel to de-risk the purchase.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Dense utility footer (support, policies, referral, account) closing the long merchandising scroll.

principle.title: Let appetite, not spec, do the selling.
principle.explanation: For a sensory product, styled in-use photography converts better than feature lists; the muted UI exists precisely to make that imagery and the price math pop.
principle.howToApply: On a DTC page, shoot the product in use (food in the pan, the object mid-action), keep the chrome muted, and make value literal with set-savings + strikethrough rather than adjectives.

entries.tagline: A widescreen billboard that carries the whole unit on one joke and one rainbow product shot - white space does the rest.
entries.summary: FinalStraw's 970x250 uses its width as a reading path: the product anchors the left, a mock-grandiose all-caps quote runs across the center, and the brand + CTA close it bottom-right. The rainbow-anodized straw and coral case are the only color against pure white, so the eye lands instantly, and the wit ('the greatest advancement in sucking technology, since, like, forever') rewards the read.
whatWorks: Uses the 970x250 width as a left-to-right read: product anchor left, big headline center, brand + CTA right.
The product is the only color - a rainbow-anodized straw and coral case against pure white - so the eye lands on it instantly.
Humor is the hook: a mock-grandiose all-caps quote rewards anyone who actually reads it.
Generous white space signals confidence and keeps a wide format from feeling crowded.
One light-blue 'Shop Now' pill is the single colored CTA, placed bottom-right where the read ends.

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The rainbow straw and coral case pop against white and stop the scan; the wide format reads like a mini-poster.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: A tongue-in-cheek grandiose quote set big in all-caps; the wit is the message, not a feature list.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: The product photographed edge-on at the left as the anchor, with white space carrying the composition across the width.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A light-blue 'Shop Now' pill sits bottom-right, exactly where the left-to-right read finishes.

principle.title: Compose a wide banner as a left-to-right sentence.
principle.explanation: A 970x250 billboard fails if you center everything; its width is a reading path - anchor the eye left, deliver the idea in the middle, and land the CTA where the scan ends.
principle.howToApply: For any wide banner, place the product or hook at the left edge, run the headline across the center, and put the CTA bottom-right; let generous white space and a single color source keep it uncluttered.

entries.tagline: The designer who embraced the Macintosh as an art tool first - inventing a layered, hybrid, unmistakably digital visual language.
entries.summary: Greiman brought Swiss New Wave typography to the US and then, in the mid-1980s, became one of the first designers to treat the Macintosh (and video and synthesizers) as a serious creative medium rather than a threat. Her 'typelayering' stacks type, photography, color and texture into deep, dynamic compositions, and her Design Quarterly 133 life-size digital self-portrait is a landmark of digital-native design. The lesson is her posture toward tools: curiosity over constraint.
whatWorks: Treat a new tool as design research - she explored the Mac, video and synths to find their native look instead of mimicking print.
Layer type, image, color and texture in depth ('typelayering') to build space and dynamism, not flat composition.
Let content and medium set the form rather than a fixed modernist rulebook.
Think transmedia - refuse the 'graphic designer' box so a poster can become a room-scale fold-out or a video.
Make the tool's artifacts (pixels, low-res, video scan lines) part of the aesthetic instead of hiding them.

sections.name: How she begins
sections.notes: By experimenting with the medium itself - asking what a new technology can do that older tools cannot - and letting that drive the piece.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Layering and spatial collage: sandwiching type, photos, color fields and texture to create depth and movement ('typelayering').

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Hybrid imagery fusing photography, video stills and digital artifacts into one composition - epitomized by her life-size digital self-portrait for Design Quarterly 133.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Curiosity over constraint; embrace emerging tech rather than fear it; let the medium's native qualities show rather than smoothing them away.

principle.title: Adopt a new tool to find its native look, not to fake the old one.
principle.explanation: Greiman's edge came from probing what the Mac and video could uniquely do and building an aesthetic from it - years before most designers trusted the machine.
principle.howToApply: When a new tool lands (an AI image model, a motion library, a 3D engine), spend a session probing what only it can do and design from that, instead of using it to reproduce what you already make by hand.

entries.tagline: A nonprofit that turns radical accountability into its entire design language — “Give water. Get proof. Every single time.”
entries.summary: charity: water's homepage is built to defeat the two things that kill donations: doubt about where the money goes, and vagueness about impact. It answers both with structure — a warm, face-forward hero, then a wall of hard counts (209,241 projects, 29 countries, 21.6M people), then an explicit “100% of your donation funds water projects” promise, then literal proof (GPS coordinates and photos of every project). The page reads less like a charity brochure and more like a receipt you can trust.
whatWorks: Human hero: a single child looking straight down the lens holding a glass of water — one honest face beats any illustration or stock montage
Impact stated as precise odometer-style counts (209,241 not ‘200k+’) — exact numbers read as audited fact, round numbers read as marketing
The transparency promise (‘100% of your donation’) is repeated at every conversion moment, not buried in an About page
Proof as a feature: ‘Give water. Get proof.’ with GPS + photos converts an abstract donation into a verifiable transaction
A giving-pathways grid (The Spring monthly, sponsor a project, fundraise, honor someone, legacy) meets each donor at their own commitment level
Trust is closed out in the footer with real seals — Candid Platinum, Charity Navigator 4-star, BBB — plus EIN and address, the credentials skeptics look for

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: Full-bleed photo of a smiling child with a glass of clean water, gazing into the camera; big confident headline ‘Bring clean and safe water to every person on the planet’ with one primary button, GIVE TODAY. Emotional but not manipulative — hope, not guilt.

sections.name: Time-boxed campaign callout
sections.notes: A slim banner above the fold (‘Give by this Sunday…’) adds a deadline and a specific current campaign, creating urgency without hijacking the main story.

sections.name: Impact statistics
sections.notes: A multi-column band of large, precise numbers — projects funded, countries, people served — each with an expandable ‘the details’ so the confident headline number is backed by methodology for anyone who wants it.

sections.name: Why water / value prop
sections.notes: ‘Clean water changes everything’ broken into health, education, women, economy — tying a single tangible gift to compounding outcomes, and restating the 100%-to-projects promise right where interest peaks.

sections.name: Proof & accountability
sections.notes: ‘Give water. Get proof. Every single time.’ — the differentiator: GPS coordinates and photos of funded projects. This is the section that converts skeptics; it treats verification as the product.

sections.name: Giving pathways grid
sections.notes: A card grid of ~8 ways to give (The Spring monthly membership, sponsor a project, start or back a fundraiser, give in honor/memory, legacy, brand partnership). One page serves the $20/mo donor and the corporate partner without splitting the funnel.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Dense but ordered — four link columns, social icons, and a trust wall of charity-rating seals plus legal identity (501(c)(3), EIN, registered addresses in US/UK/NL). Closes the credibility loop for due-diligence donors.

principle.title: Make proof the product, not a promise
principle.explanation: Trust-dependent conversions (donations, high-consideration purchases, B2B signups) fail on unspoken doubt. charity: water wins by pre-empting every objection with verifiable evidence — exact counts, an unambiguous money promise, GPS-tagged photos, and third-party seals — so believing them requires no leap of faith.
principle.howToApply: On any page asking for trust, list the top three doubts a skeptic has and answer each with something checkable, not adjectives: precise numbers over ‘thousands’, a screenshot/receipt/coordinate over ‘transparent’, a named third-party badge over ‘trusted’. Repeat the single strongest guarantee at every CTA, not once.

site.name: PNC Bank — animated display banner (analogy hook)

entries.tagline: An animated banner that sells mobile banking sideways — through a relatable everyday analogy instead of a feature list.
entries.summary: Rather than lead with rates or app features, PNC opens on an unexpected premise — ‘If your phone makes it easy to monitor your dog…’ over footage of a pet cam — and lets the animation complete the thought toward effortless mobile banking. The analogy does the persuading: it borrows a familiar, pleasant everyday convenience and transfers that feeling onto a boring category. Restrained type, a single continuous visual, and a quiet brand close keep the small portrait unit from feeling cluttered.
whatWorks: Curiosity-gap hook: the headline is an unfinished ‘If…’ sentence, so the eye stays to see the payoff — motion earns the extra second a static banner can't
Analogy over features: it never says ‘low fees’; it borrows the ease of a pet cam and lets you map it onto banking yourself
One continuous visual (a phone held in-hand showing the dog) — a single focal point, no competing product shots
Type restraint: short all-caps lines animate in sequence, so each beat is readable at mobile size before the next arrives
Quiet, confident close on the PNC lockup with an honest disclaimer (‘Available in eligible markets only’) rather than a shouting button

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The scroll-stopper is a question you can't finish in your head: ‘IF YOUR PHONE MAKES IT EASY TO MONITOR YOUR DOG…’. Ellipsis + animation = an open loop the viewer waits to close.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: The whole unit is one sentence delivered in animated beats, resolving the dog-cam premise into ‘…it should make banking just as easy.’ Persuasion by transferred feeling, not spec sheet.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: A single warm lifestyle shot — a hand holding a phone streaming the dog — anchors the analogy literally. One image, one action, no clutter; the phone is both the subject and the product metaphor.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: Understated: the payoff frame lands on the PNC Bank lockup with a small disclaimer, trusting the brand + resolved analogy to drive the click. Effective for brand recall, though a more explicit ‘Explore mobile banking’ button would likely lift direct response — the one place the unit leaves conversion on the table.

principle.title: Sell a boring product through a borrowed feeling
principle.explanation: Low-interest categories (banking, insurance, utilities) can't win attention on features. PNC transfers the warmth of an everyday convenience onto banking via analogy, and uses animation to hold the viewer through the reveal — so the ad earns emotion a feature list never could.
principle.howToApply: For a dull product, find a vivid, universally-loved everyday moment that shares its core benefit (ease, safety, speed), open on that moment, and let the audience complete the mapping. In motion formats, phrase the hook as an unfinished sentence so the animation has a payoff to deliver.

entries.tagline: Co-designed Britain's entire road-sign system — proof that the most-seen design in a country can be warm, humane, and tested against real eyes.
entries.summary: With Jock Kinneir, Margaret Calvert designed the signage that still governs UK roads, motorways, airports and rail — including the Transport typeface and the friendly pictograms (the schoolchildren, the farm animals). Their method was radically user-first for the 1950s: legibility tested empirically, mixed-case lettering chosen because word-shapes read faster at speed, and every symbol drawn from lived, ordinary experience. It's a masterclass in designing systems that millions must understand instantly, under stress, at 70mph.
whatWorks: Design for the worst-case reader, not the ideal one — they optimized for a tired driver at speed, so the everyday case became effortless
Test legibility empirically: prototypes were trialed for reading distance and speed, and colour combinations chosen from research (black-on-yellow at Gatwick), not taste
Choose mixed-case over all-caps because the human eye reads word-shapes, not letters — a data-backed break from convention
Root symbols in lived experience: the farm-animals sign came from a real cow (Patience), the schoolchildren from herself — specificity makes pictograms instantly legible
Build a system, not signs: one typeface + a consistent grammar (triangles warn, circles command) so any new sign is generated by rules, not reinvented
The ‘would my aunt understand this?’ test — judge comprehension by an ordinary non-expert, not a designer

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: Start from the reading conditions, not the aesthetics: who sees this, how fast, how stressed, from how far? Kinneir & Calvert defined the human performance problem (legibility at speed and distance) before drawing anything, then let those constraints generate the form.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Empirical, systematic iteration — build prototypes, test them against real reading distances and speeds, and standardise the winner into a reusable system: a single typeface (Transport), a fixed colour logic, and spacing rules so the whole national set stays coherent as it grows.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Humanist pictograms drawn from real, specific life (a named cow; herself as a child) rendered in the simplest legible form, paired with mixed-case type chosen because word-shapes are recognised faster than strings of capitals.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Comprehension by an ordinary person is the only success metric — the ‘would my aunt understand this’ heuristic. Innovate inside inherited protocols (European sign conventions) rather than for novelty; warmth and clarity are not opposites.

principle.title: Design for the reader at their worst moment
principle.explanation: Calvert optimized signage for a tired driver at speed — the hardest reading condition — and everything easier came for free. Pairing that with empirical legibility testing and symbols drawn from lived experience produced a system that's both humane and machine-reliable across millions of daily readings.
principle.howToApply: Before designing any UI or wayfinding, name the worst realistic reading condition (small screen, glance-length attention, stress, low vision) and design for that; then validate with a real non-expert instead of your own eye — pick mixed-case, tested contrast, and concrete familiar icons over clever abstract ones.

entries.tagline: Scandinavian editorial restraint applied to car marketing — oversized type-as-navigation and hairline rules turn a dealer site into an object you want to scroll.
entries.summary: Polestar treats an automotive marketing site the way a design house treats a magazine: a strict monochrome palette, generous whitespace, thin hairline dividers, and one recurring move — huge display type used AS the navigation. The page alternates deliberately between full-bleed cinematic imagery (dark, moody car-in-context shots) and product-on-white studio frames, so the rhythm never flattens. It sells clarity by being clear; the restraint IS the brand argument for a 'clean' EV.
whatWorks: Type-as-navigation: a stack of oversized links (“Book a test drive / View offers / Find a retailer”) with trailing chevrons replaces a button row — the headline and the CTA are the same object
Ruthless monochrome: near-black, white and grey everywhere, so the only saturated color (orange charging cables, EV-calculator parking lines) reads as functional information, not decoration

Deliberate light/dark rhythm: cinematic dark hero → product-on-white model row → white type block → airy sustainability render → dark aerial charging shot — alternation keeps a long scroll from going monotone
Hairline dividers and a strict column grid do all the sectioning; there are almost no boxes, cards, or shadows
Ghosted outline CTAs over imagery (thin 1px borders, no fill) keep the photography the hero while still offering an action
The nav bar is a single thin line of small text — the chrome recedes so the content carries the page

sections.name: Header / nav
sections.notes: One hairline-thin row: wordmark left, small-caps model + ownership links right, location and account icons. Almost weightless — the chrome gets out of the way so imagery and type own the viewport.

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: Full-bleed cinematic shot of the Polestar 4 on a city street at dusk, white overlay text (“Summer of Clarity Event / Lease from $579/mo”) and two ghosted outline CTAs. Sells mood and context first, spec second.

sections.name: Two-up model row
sections.notes: Polestar 3 and Polestar 2 shown side-by-side as studio shots on pure white, each with a short offer line and text links. The switch from dark hero to bright white is what makes the products pop.

sections.name: Type-as-navigation block
sections.notes: A white section with three enormous stacked links — Book a test drive / View offers / Find a retailer — each with a chevron. Navigation rendered at editorial-headline scale; the wayfinding is the visual.

sections.name: Sustainability & Charging
sections.notes: Airy sections pairing restrained body copy with a papercraft-style renewable-city render, then a big ‘↗ Charging’ headline. Brand values get the same typographic dignity as the product.

sections.name: EV Savings Calculator
sections.notes: Dark aerial photo of two cars charging in a lot, orange parking outlines guiding the eye; a single ‘Run the numbers’ CTA. The one place saturated color appears — and it's carrying meaning.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Newsletter subscribe prompt, then four tidy columns (Explore & buy / Shop / About / Social) over a long legal block and a country selector. Dense but ordered on the same column grid — restraint holds even in the fine print.

principle.title: Let type be the navigation
principle.explanation: When a palette is this restrained, scale becomes the only hierarchy you need. Rendering primary actions as oversized headline-scale links (not buttons) makes the wayfinding double as the visual design — fewer elements, more confidence, and the page reads as editorial rather than as a form.
principle.howToApply: On a landing page with a spare palette, try replacing a row of CTA buttons with a short stack of very large text links plus a trailing chevron. Keep them on white with a hairline divider above; let the size carry the emphasis instead of a colored fill.

entries.tagline: A split-panel billboard that does one job: a struck-through price, a deadline, and a single blue button.
entries.when: GDN display banner, ~2019–2020 (Nest Mini promotional pricing)
entries.summary: This is a masterclass in fitting a message to a wide, short format. The 970×250 billboard splits cleanly in two: a white left panel carries all the words, a photo panel on the right carries the product. The price does the persuading — ‘$29 $49’ with the old number struck through — and ‘ends tomorrow.’ supplies the urgency, so the copy can stay almost nothing. One product, one price, one button.
whatWorks: Hard split-panel layout: words left, product right — the format's width is used as a divider instead of being fought
Price anchoring as the headline: ‘$29’ next to a struck-through ‘$49’ makes the discount the biggest visual, no adjectives required
One-word urgency: ‘ends tomorrow.’ does the scarcity work in two syllables
A single high-contrast blue ‘Buy now’ button is the only saturated element — the eye lands on the action last, by design
Product shot on a calm green field with soft light — the Nest Mini is unmistakable and uncluttered, no competing props
Brand lockup (Google ‘G’ + Nest) sits quiet top-left; the deal, not the logo, is the hero

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The struck-through price. In a wide banner glimpsed for a fraction of a second, a big number crossed out and replaced by a smaller one reads instantly as ‘deal’ before a single word is processed.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: ‘$29 $49 ends tomorrow.’ plus a two-line subhead (‘Best deal of the year on Nest Mini.’). The offer is the headline; there is no brand slogan competing for attention.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: A clean product photo of the Nest Mini on green fabric fills the right panel — one object, generous negative space, soft shadow. It shows exactly what $29 buys.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A rounded blue ‘Buy now’ button — the only saturated, high-contrast element in the unit, sized to be tappable and placed at the end of the reading path.

principle.title: Let the price be the headline
principle.explanation: For a transactional display unit, a struck-through price plus a deadline out-persuades any clever copy line. It's pre-attentive (a crossed-out number reads as ‘discount’ instantly), it's concrete, and it frees the rest of the unit to stay calm — one product, one button.
principle.howToApply: On a promo banner or pricing hero, set the deal itself at headline scale: new price large, old price struck through beside it, one short urgency phrase, and a single high-contrast CTA. Cut the descriptive tagline — the numbers are doing the selling.

entries.tagline: Turns a script or a brief into a single kinetic sculpture the audience moves through — design as the emotional argument of the piece.
entries.summary: Es Devlin is a British stage and installation designer who reframed set design as sculpture: not scenery behind the action but the active, moving idea of the piece. She works across opera, theatre, stadium tours, and public art (the UK's Expo 2020 ‘Poem Pavilion’, Trafalgar Square's Fifth Lion), and her process — read deeply, draw obsessively, reduce to one governing object — is unusually transferable to any design discipline.
whatWorks: Read before you draw: she immerses in the source text/subject first, so the form is an argument about meaning, not decoration
Keep a daily drawing notebook — thinking happens in the hand, and ideas are found on paper before they're built
Reduce a whole work to ONE governing object or gesture (a revolving cube, a mirrored room, a singing tree) that carries the entire concept
Design what the piece NEEDS, not what it wants — interrogate the brief for its underlying emotional requirement, not its literal ask
Make the audience a participant, not a spectator: build sets that move, respond, and invite contribution
Use light, mirror, and scale as primary materials — emotion is engineered through space, not surface detail

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: With research and reading, not sketching a set. She saturates herself in the text or subject until a central metaphor surfaces, then draws constantly — the notebook is where the concept is discovered, not just recorded.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Distil the entire work to a single sculptural idea — ‘a dream-like constellation of things,’ in her words, where ‘the piece happens between those objects.’ She designs the spatial relationship and the emotional journey, then lets detail follow the governing gesture.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Kinetic, sculptural sets built from unexpected materials (mirror sheeting, plastic, string, projection mapping) that rotate, open, and respond to the performance — and increasingly public works that literally ask the audience to contribute (messages to space at Expo 2020).

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: ‘Design what a play needs, not what it wants.’ A set must be active, never passive scenery. If an element doesn't carry meaning or move the emotion, it's cut — the test is resonance, not literal accuracy.

principle.title: Reduce the whole thing to one governing object
principle.explanation: Devlin's power move is compression: an entire opera or brand story collapsed into a single sculptural idea that everything else serves. A design with one clear central gesture is more memorable and more coherent than one with many competing good ideas.
principle.howToApply: Before designing a page or campaign, force the brief down to one governing image or gesture and write it in a sentence. Make every section serve that one idea; if an element doesn't reinforce it, cut it. Start in a notebook by hand before touching the tool.

entries.tagline: A wearable brand that sells trust by staging the ring inside real daily moments, not spec sheets.
entries.summary: Oura's homepage treats a tiny piece of hardware as a lifestyle system. It opens on the smallest-ever ring shot in near-empty space, then moves quickly from 'what it is' to 'what it does for your day' — walking you through waking, moving, getting sick, winding down. Lifestyle photography is always paired with the actual app UI so the promise and the proof sit side by side, and FSA/HSA + payment cues de-risk the buy at the end.
whatWorks: Hero sells the object with restraint — 'Subtle. Power.' plus one clean ring shot on empty ground; the product's smallness IS the message.
A day-in-the-life module (waking, walking, under the weather, winding down, hosting) frames features as moments, so buyers picture the ring in their own routine.
Every lifestyle photo is twinned with a real app-UI screenshot — aspiration and evidence in the same frame, never one without the other.
The membership carousel splits value into six bodies of concern (Sleep, Heart, Women's Health, Stress…) so each visitor self-selects the reason that matters to them.
FSA/HSA eligibility surfaced prominently in the payments block quietly reframes price as health spend.

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: 'Subtle. Power.' with 'the world's smallest smart ring is here' and one focal ring shot plus 'Explore'. Emptiness signals confidence; the CTA is soft (Explore, not Buy).

sections.name: Product showcase
sections.notes: Two model cards (Ring 4, Ring 4 Ceramic Midnight) with Shop CTAs — the range shown at a glance before deep storytelling.

sections.name: Membership value
sections.notes: 'Oura Membership gives your body a voice' — a six-scenario carousel (Sleep, Wellness/Longevity, Activity, Heart, Women's Health, Stress) letting visitors pick an entry point.

sections.name: Form & function
sections.notes: 'Built for accuracy, designed for your everyday' — five day-in-the-life modules pairing lifestyle photography with the matching app screen and a member quote.

sections.name: In the news
sections.notes: A large lifestyle image plus CNN / CNBC / Cosmopolitan mentions as linked headlines — third-party credibility.

sections.name: Payment & trust
sections.notes: Two-column payment methods leading with FSA/HSA eligibility, then Affirm/Klarna/PayPal, wallets, and cards — de-risking the purchase.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Compliance/legal links, privacy notice, and language selector — quiet and orderly.

principle.title: Pair every aspiration with its proof in the same frame
principle.explanation: Lifestyle imagery sells desire but not credibility; a product screenshot sells credibility but not desire. Oura never shows one without the other, so each scroll delivers both the feeling and the evidence at once.
principle.howToApply: On any product page, template feature blocks as a pair: one emotive lifestyle image plus the literal UI/output it produces, side by side. Never let a mood shot stand alone or a raw screenshot float without human context.

entries.tagline: A pure brand-yellow field, one serif promise, one button — the whole unit is a single confident statement.
entries.summary: This is Mailchimp reduced to its atoms: the unmistakable Cavendish yellow filling the frame, a benefit headline in their editorial serif, a single teal button, and Freddie winking at the bottom. No product shot, no clutter — brand color does the recognition work and the copy does the selling. It's a masterclass in how much a strong brand can strip away.
whatWorks: The saturated brand yellow fills the entire unit — instant recognition before a word is read, and it stops the scroll by sheer color.
One benefit headline ('Create email campaigns that get more clicks') set in Mailchimp's serif — specific outcome, no feature list.
A single teal 'Sign Up Now' button is the only other color, so the one action is unmissable.
Freddie plus the wordmark anchor the base, locking in attribution without a logo competing with the message.
Radical restraint: no imagery, no secondary copy — proof a confident brand can win a banner on color plus one line.

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The flat Cavendish-yellow field — a color so owned by Mailchimp it works as the scroll-stopper on its own.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'Create email campaigns that get more clicks.' — outcome-led, plain-spoken, in the brand serif for warmth.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: No product shot at all; the yellow void IS the visual. Freddie the chimp winks at the bottom as the only illustration.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A solid teal 'Sign Up Now' rectangle — the single non-yellow element, so the eye lands on it last and clicks.

principle.title: Let an owned brand color do the work of a visual
principle.explanation: When a brand truly owns a color, that color alone provides recognition and stopping power — freeing the unit to spend its space on one clear line and one clear action instead of a busy hero image.
principle.howToApply: For a strong-brand banner, run the one-color test: fill the frame with the brand's signature hue, add a single benefit line and one contrasting button, and cut every image. If it still reads instantly, the image was decoration.

entries.category: Concrete art / Swiss design · Ulm School
entries.tagline: The polymath who made mathematics visible — designing objects, buildings, type and art from a single systematic logic.
entries.summary: Max Bill was a Bauhaus-trained architect, artist, and industrial/graphic designer who co-founded the Ulm School of Design (HfG Ulm) and championed 'concrete art' — art built from mathematical and geometric law rather than depiction. Across a stool, a wall clock, a poster and a sculpture he applied the same conviction: form should be the honest, inevitable result of a governing rule. He is the bridge from the Bauhaus to the systematic Swiss method. (Image: his 'Endless Ribbon' sculpture, a signature work.)
whatWorks: Start from a governing mathematical rule (a progression, a geometric operation) and let the form be its output — design as the visible result of logic.
Work across media from one system — the same proportional discipline scales from typography to product to architecture.
Treat 'concrete' construction as honesty: no illusion, no ornament; every element earns its place by the rule.
Use the grid and mathematical proportion as generative tools, not just alignment aids — they produce the composition, they don't merely tidy it.
Design the whole environment (Ulm's curriculum, buildings, and products together) as one coherent, teachable method.

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: With an underlying rule or mathematical idea rather than a sketch of the final look — 'what law governs this?' — then derives form from it.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Systematic construction: geometric progressions, ratios and grids used generatively, so composition is calculated and inevitable rather than intuitive.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: 'Concrete art' — abstract works and objects whose form is purely the expression of mathematical/geometric relationships (e.g. his endless-ribbon and progression sculptures).

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Reject decoration and illusion; a form is right when it is the honest, economical consequence of its rule. Good design is teachable and unified across all media.

principle.title: Derive the form from a rule, don't decorate a shape
principle.explanation: Bill's power is that composition is an output, not a taste call. When a layout or object is generated by an explicit system (a ratio, a progression, a grid logic), it gains an internal inevitability that ad-hoc arrangement can't fake — and it stays coherent across every size and medium.
principle.howToApply: Before styling a layout, write the rule that governs it — a modular scale, a column math, a spacing ratio — and let that rule place things. If you can't state the rule, you're decorating; find the rule first.

entries.tagline: A clinical product made approachable — soft illustration and a 'what do you need?' router turn therapy into something friendly.
entries.summary: Headspace has the hard job of making mental-health care feel warm and low-stakes. Its homepage does that with rounded, soft-hued illustration, a plain benefit headline, and — crucially — an intent router ('What kind of headspace are you looking for?') that lets each visitor self-select a path (stress, sleep, anxiety, therapy) instead of being pushed down one funnel. Enterprise proof and a tabbed content library follow, and an FAQ absorbs objections before the footer.
whatWorks: An intent router of six buttons (Stress less, Sleep soundly, Manage anxiety, Process thoughts, Practice meditation, Start therapy) segments visitors by need in one tap.
'Try for $0' with HSA/FSA eligibility reframes a subscription as a health benefit and removes price friction.
Soft, rounded illustration plus calm photography set an emotional tone that matches the promise — the visual language IS the value prop.
Enterprise credibility ('Over 4,000 leading organizations') with a logo carousel placed after the emotional pitch, converting warmth into trust.
A tabbed content library previews real product depth, including the Ebb AI companion and Cocomelon partner content.

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: 'Stress less all with Headspace' plus 'Try for $0' with an HSA/FSA note; a secondary hero promotes insurance-accepted online therapy.

sections.name: Intent router
sections.notes: 'What kind of headspace are you looking for?' — six need-based buttons let visitors self-select their path.

sections.name: Social proof / enterprise
sections.notes: 'Members are enjoying happier and healthier lives', then 'Over 4,000 leading organizations choose Headspace' with a client logo carousel.

sections.name: Content library
