sections.notes: Tabbed showcase (Featured, Popular, Sleep, Stress, Meditation) with cards — the Ebb AI companion and a Cocomelon partnership on display.

sections.name: FAQ
sections.notes: Nine expandable questions on basics, subscription and cancellation — objections handled inline without visual clutter.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Four columns (Get some Headspace, Our content, About us, Support) plus app badges and legal links.

principle.title: Open with an intent router, not a single funnel
principle.explanation: When your audience arrives with different problems, forcing one path loses the rest. A 'what do you need?' selector near the top lets each visitor route themselves, so the very next screen already feels personalized — higher relevance, higher conversion.
principle.howToApply: On a multi-audience landing page, add a set of need-based entry buttons just under the hero and wire each to a tailored section or page. Name them by the visitor's problem ('Sleep soundly'), not your feature ('Sleepcasts').

entries.tagline: Logo, one universal benefit line, one green button — a square that says everything a merchant needs in three beats.
entries.summary: Shopify's square banner is textbook restraint on a small canvas: a warm off-white ground, the green bag logo up top, a single sweeping benefit ('Everything you need to sell online'), and a rounded green 'Try it free today' button. There's no product screenshot to shrink into illegibility — at 300×250 the copy and CTA get all the room, and the free framing kills risk.
whatWorks: A single, all-encompassing promise ('Everything you need to sell online') does the work of a feature list in a space too small for one.
A warm off-white ground keeps the tiny unit calm and legible instead of shouting — contrast is spent on the button, not the background.
The rounded green 'Try it free today' button is the one saturated element and the clear focal point.
The bag-logo plus wordmark up top gives instant attribution without stealing space from the message.
'Free' in the CTA lowers the barrier — exactly the right ask for a top-of-funnel display impression.

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The clean green Shopify bag mark on calm cream — brand-forward and uncluttered, it reads as trustworthy at a glance.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'Everything you need to sell online' — one benefit that contains the whole product, ideal for 300×250.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: No product shot; whitespace and the logo carry it, keeping the small unit uncrowded and readable.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A rounded green 'Try it free today' pill — the single saturated element, risk-free framing, thumb-sized.

principle.title: On a small unit, sell the whole product in one line
principle.explanation: A 300×250 has no room for a feature list or a legible screenshot. One sweeping benefit that stands in for everything (plus a free CTA) converts better than three cramped selling points fighting for the same 250 pixels.
principle.howToApply: When designing small banners, write the single sentence that would make someone click if it were the only thing they read; drop the product shot if it can't be legible, and spend the saved space on whitespace and the button.

entries.tagline: The Constructivist who fused photography, type and geometry into 'production art' — design as a tool with a job, not a picture to admire.
entries.summary: Rodchenko abandoned painting to become an engineer of images: posters, book covers, ad graphics and photomontage built from photography, bold geometric shapes, diagonal dynamism and hard-working typography. With the poet Mayakovsky he ran the 'Reklam-Konstruktor' studio, treating advertising as a constructive craft. He also revolutionized photography with extreme angles (the 'Rodchenko perspective') to make the everyday newly legible.
whatWorks: Build with photography instead of illustration — real photographic fragments in montage read as modern, factual and immediate.
Use strong diagonals and geometric blocks to create movement and hierarchy on a flat page — energy from structure, not ornament.
Integrate type as a structural element (bars, arrows, size jumps) that directs the eye, not just labels the image.
Shoot from radical angles (worm's-eye, bird's-eye) to defamiliarize a subject and force fresh attention.
Treat design as 'production art' — every piece is a functional tool made for a task, judged by whether it works, not whether it's pretty.

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: From the communicative job to be done — what must this poster or cover make the viewer do or see? — then assembles from photographic and geometric parts, not a drawn composition.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Photomontage and construction: cut, scale and layer photographic fragments with geometric shapes and type into a dynamic, purposeful whole.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Aggressive diagonals plus the 'Rodchenko perspective' (extreme camera angles), and type set as bold structural bars and arrows.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Reject 'pure' art for utility — design serves a social/commercial function; a work succeeds if it communicates and mobilizes, and dynamism beats symmetry.

principle.title: Compose with real fragments and diagonals, not tidy symmetry
principle.explanation: Rodchenko got energy and credibility by building from photographic reality and driving the eye with diagonal structure. Montaged real elements feel factual and modern, and an off-axis, dynamic layout commands attention that centered symmetry can't.
principle.howToApply: For a bold editorial or campaign layout, assemble from real photographic pieces rather than stock illustration, and set a strong diagonal or angular grid to lead the eye; use type as structural bars and arrows that point, not just caption.

entries.tagline: A B2B homepage written like a straight-talking letter — the founder's voice, not a feature matrix, does the selling.
entries.summary: Basecamp's homepage is a masterclass in voice-led B2B. Instead of a slick feature grid, it leads with a plainspoken promise, then a conversational 'Tell me if this sounds about right' section where founder Jason Fried names the customer's pain in human language. Twenty years of trust are proved with a wall of real customer quotes and staggering 'big numbers', and even the support team is sold as people with names and tenure. The design is quiet and typographic on purpose — the copy is the product demo.
whatWorks: A founder's-voice value prop ('Tell me if this sounds about right') names the buyer's pain conversationally — empathy sells before features do.
A dense wall of short, attributed customer quotes across varied industries reads as breadth of trust at a glance.
'Big numbers. Highly-trusted' (84M accounts, 1.7B to-dos, 99.99% uptime) turns 20 years into hard, scannable proof.
Support sold as humans — 'Remember when companies cared about service?' with a named person and a 9-year average tenure — a differentiator most SaaS buries in a help center.
A deliberately quiet, typographic layout with generous whitespace keeps the opinionated copy the hero, not decoration.

sections.name: Hero
sections.notes: 'The refreshingly straightforward project management system…' plus a 3-minute walkthrough video over a real project screenshot — clarity over spectacle.

sections.name: Value proposition
sections.notes: 'Tell me if this sounds about right' — Jason Fried's personal letter naming the scattered-tools problem and Basecamp's 20-year answer.

sections.name: Social proof
sections.notes: 10+ short attributed customer quotes from marketing, nonprofits, universities and churches — diverse, human, scannable.

sections.name: Big numbers
sections.notes: 'Big numbers. Highly-trusted' — 84M accounts, 60M projects, 1.7B to-dos, 99.99% uptime as trust evidence.

sections.name: Service / culture
sections.notes: 'Remember when companies cared about service?' with a video of Chase, Head of Support, and a 9-year average tenure.

sections.name: Features
sections.notes: 'All these questions have the same answer: Yes!' — 21 capability questions answered in one move, linking to the full feature list.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Extensive links, company values, and cross-links to HEY and Fizzy.

principle.title: Sell in the founder's voice, not the feature matrix
principle.explanation: A distinctive, plainspoken voice that names the customer's pain builds trust and differentiation a spec grid never can — especially for a mature product whose edge is philosophy, not features. Copy becomes the demo.
principle.howToApply: On a value-prop section, write the pain in the customer's own words as if speaking one-to-one ('Tell me if this sounds about right…') before listing any capability. Keep the layout quiet and typographic so the voice carries.

entries.tagline: A dev-infra product made charming — a specific speed claim plus a flying-mail illustration turn deliverability into delight.
entries.summary: SendGrid takes a dry developer utility — email deliverability — and makes it likeable in a tall, narrow unit. A pale-blue sky, the Twilio SendGrid lockup, and a single concrete claim ('gets to the inbox in 2.6 seconds') sit above a playful illustration of a figure speeding through the clouds in a red car trailing envelopes. The oddly specific number does the persuading; the illustration carries the brand's warmth in a format that usually feels utilitarian.
whatWorks: An oddly specific claim ('2.6 seconds') reads as true and technical — precision is more persuasive than a round 'fast'.
A warm, whimsical illustration humanizes an infrastructure product and fills the awkward tall skyscraper shape without stretching a photo.
The sky-blue palette with a mail metaphor makes the abstract benefit (deliverability) instantly legible as a picture.
A clean top lockup gives Twilio/SendGrid attribution while the vertical space is used as a narrative (headline up top, journey below).
The AdChoices marker and full-bleed clickable creative show it's built as a real GDN unit, not a portfolio mock.

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The pale-blue cloud sky and a red car mid-flight — an unexpected, friendly scene that stops the eye in a column of text.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'Email delivery service that gets to the inbox in 2.6 seconds.' — one concrete, quantified promise.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: A figure driving a red convertible through the clouds trailing envelopes — the deliverability benefit rendered as a single memorable picture, sized to fill the 160×600.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: No explicit button; the whole banner is the click target, with the specific speed claim carrying the persuasion — a reminder that a strong claim can be its own CTA.

principle.title: Use an oddly specific number as your proof
principle.explanation: A precise figure ('2.6 seconds') is far more believable than a vague superlative ('lightning fast') — specificity signals a real measurement stands behind the claim, which is exactly what a technical buyer trusts.
principle.howToApply: Replace generic speed/scale adjectives in headlines with the actual measured number wherever you have one; if you don't have it, go measure it. Precision reads as honesty.

entries.tagline: The studio that industrialized identity — turning brand design into a documented, repeatable process and one of Europe's largest independent firms.
entries.summary: MetaDesign, shaped by co-founder Erik Spiekermann (pictured) in Berlin, made its name building rigorous, systematic corporate identities (Berlin's BVG transit system, Deutsche Bank, Audi, Volkswagen, Adobe) and codifying them into exhaustive guidelines and custom typefaces. Its operating model treated identity not as a logo but as a complete, documented system a large organization could run for decades — process and consistency over one-off flair.
whatWorks: Design the system, not the logo — deliverables are complete identity toolkits (type, color, grid, usage rules) an organization can operate itself.
Commission or draw a custom typeface as the identity's backbone — proprietary type does more brand work than a mark alone (e.g. FF Meta, transit faces).
Document everything: exhaustive guidelines make consistency survive staff turnover and scale across a huge org.
Structure the studio around process and multidisciplinary teams so large, long-running programs are delivered repeatably, not heroically.
Solve real wayfinding/functional problems (transit maps, signage) as design — usefulness is the brief, decoration a by-product.

sections.name: How it's structured
sections.notes: A large, process-driven, multidisciplinary firm (Berlin-founded, later international) built to deliver big corporate-identity programs repeatably rather than depend on a single star's hand.

sections.name: How it takes on work
sections.notes: Engages major institutions and corporations needing a coherent system at scale — transit authorities, banks, carmakers, software — where consistency across thousands of touchpoints is the real problem.

sections.name: Signature method
sections.notes: Systematic identity: define a custom typeface, grid, color and usage logic, then capture it in comprehensive guidelines and asset systems the client can run for years.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Clarity and function first (Spiekermann's typographic rigor); an identity is judged by how consistently and usefully it performs in the wild, not by novelty.

principle.title: Ship the system and the manual, not just the mark
principle.explanation: A logo is a moment; a documented system is what actually holds a brand together across a large organization and many years. MetaDesign's edge was treating the guidelines, type and asset kit as the real deliverable — so consistency survived scale and staff turnover.
principle.howToApply: When you design an identity, even a small one, deliver a short but real system: a type scale, color tokens, spacing/grid rules, and a one-page usage guide. Hand over the rules, not just the artwork, so it stays coherent after you leave.

entries.title: Snap a two-column article to a baseline grid in InDesign
entries.skill: Baseline-grid alignment, paragraph styles, and killing widows/orphans so body text locks to a shared rhythm across columns.
entries.brief: Set the supplied headline and body copy in a two-column text frame, turn on a baseline grid, and lock every line of body text to it — then fix widows and orphans with tracking and hyphenation, never with extra returns.
steps: New Letter doc: 2 columns, 12mm gutter, 20mm margins. Set a baseline grid at 14pt increments (Preferences ▸ Grids), starting at the top margin.
Create a Body paragraph style at 9.5/14pt and enable 'Align to Grid: All Lines' so every baseline snaps to the grid.
Create a Heading style for the headline; set 'Space After' in whole grid increments (e.g. 28pt) so the body picks the grid back up cleanly below it.
Flow the body copy into both columns and confirm the baselines align rung-for-rung across the two columns.
Hunt widows/orphans: turn on Keep Options (2 lines at start/end), then fix any single-word last lines by tightening tracking (−10 to −20) or adjusting hyphenation — not by hitting Return.
Export a PNG/PDF and check: drop a ruler guide on any baseline in column 1 — it should hit the same baseline in column 2.

assets.label: Headline
assets.value: Form is the visible result of a rule

assets.label: Body copy
assets.value: Good design rarely begins with a picture of the finished thing. It begins with a rule — a proportion, a grid, a progression — and lets that rule decide where every element goes. When the system is honest, the layout feels inevitable rather than arranged, and it holds together at any size and in any medium. Ornament is what we add when we don't trust the structure. Strip it away and the discipline shows: consistent spacing, a clear hierarchy, type that sits on a shared rhythm. The reader never notices the grid, only the calm it produces. That quiet is the whole point of setting type well.

entries.successLooksLike: A ruler dropped on any body baseline lands on a baseline in the other column too; no line of body text floats off-grid, and there are no one-word widows or single-line orphans.
entries.stretch: Add a pull-quote that spans both columns and still returns the following body text to the baseline grid.

entries.tagline: A firehose of design news made navigable — every piece is filed on two axes (discipline AND topic) and hierarchy is built from scale and space, not boxes.
entries.summary: It's Nice That publishes at high volume, so the whole site is an exercise in making a lot of content feel curated rather than overwhelming. It leans on a disciplined two-axis taxonomy (14 disciplines plus an alphabetical tag list), an hourly-refreshed 'Nice Feed' that gives the page a living pulse, and clean colored discipline badges that act as a scannable legend across every card. Hierarchy comes almost entirely from typographic scale and whitespace rather than background panels, which keeps a dense page feeling calm and editorial.
whatWorks: Two-axis taxonomy: every article is classified by discipline (Photography, Animation…) AND by topic tag, so the archive can be entered from either direction
Colored discipline badges give each card a consistent, scannable legend — you read category before headline
'The Nice Feed' — hourly micro-updates (thumb + headline + source + timestamp) — adds a living pulse without stealing focus from featured stories
The full-width spotlight uses a big serif headline on plain white; hierarchy is scale + space, not a colored box
Lowercase, utilitarian tag typography keeps a dense taxonomy visually quiet
Accessibility is built in (Recite Me toggle) rather than bolted on

sections.name: Masthead & primary nav
sections.notes: Wordmark plus four broad category pairs ('Projects + Creatives', 'Insights + Opinion', 'Resources + Advice', 'Culture + Lifestyle') and search; a secondary row surfaces standing programs (Nicer Tuesdays, Ones to Watch, Visual Research Department). Grouping pairs of ideas per nav item keeps a broad remit to four choices.

sections.name: Discipline taxonomy menu
sections.notes: A 14-discipline menu (Advertising → Publication) plus an alphabetical, lowercase 'popular tags' list. This is the two-axis IA in the open: browse by medium or by subject.

sections.name: The Nice Feed
sections.notes: Hourly-refreshed stacked cards — small thumbnail, brief headline, publication, timestamp. Information density is deliberately high here; it reads as a live ticker of the design world.

sections.name: Category article grids
sections.notes: Four sections, each showing 3–4 featured articles in a clean grid with category tags, dates, and colored discipline badges. Consistent card anatomy makes scanning across sections effortless.

sections.name: Featured spotlight
sections.notes: A full-width lead story with a large serif headline and descriptive subtext; discipline/tag badges sit below. No background color — scale and spacing alone signal 'this is the big one'.

sections.name: Footer
sections.notes: Multi-column, white sans on a dark ground: company info, category links, social icons, legal, plus a 'Network' block linking sister properties (Creative Lives in Progress, If You Could Jobs). The dark footer bookends the light editorial body.

principle.title: Give a firehose of content two ways in
principle.explanation: A high-volume archive drowns the reader unless each item is classified on more than one axis. It's Nice That tags every piece by discipline and by topic, so someone can enter by medium ('Photography') or by subject ('Branding') — and colored badges make the category legible before the headline.
principle.howToApply: On any content-heavy build (blog, docs, gallery, catalog), define two orthogonal taxonomies and expose both as filters; render one of them as consistent colored badges so category is scannable at a glance across the whole grid, and build hierarchy from type scale and whitespace instead of colored panels.

entries.tagline: Two matched two-word lines carry the whole pitch; a calm pink photograph carries the mood.
entries.summary: Klarna states 'buy now, pay later' in a single glance with a parallel headline — 'Shopping, done. Payments, later.' — then spends the rest of the unit on feeling, not features. A soft-pink monochrome palette ties the headline, the lifestyle photo, and the wordmark into one calm mood, and the hero is product-in-context: the phone in the scene is running Klarna's own app. The model's relaxed, eyes-closed posture literally performs the benefit (stress-free), and the CTA stays a quiet 'Try it now'.
whatWorks: Parallel two-word headline ('Shopping, done. Payments, later.') communicates the whole BNPL value prop pre-attentively
Exactly one benefit subline spells out the mechanic ('pay in 4 interest-free payments') — and then stops
Monochrome soft-pink palette unifies headline, photo, and logo into a single calm mood
Hero is product-in-context — the phone in the scene shows Klarna's own app, so the proof is inside the lifestyle shot
The relaxed, eyes-closed model performs the emotional benefit rather than describing it
A low-pressure 'Try it now' under the wordmark matches the brand's easygoing tone

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The wash of soft pink and a person visibly at ease stops the scroll on mood — it reads as calm before you read a word.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: Two symmetrical two-word fragments in bold black set the top of the unit; the parallel cadence makes the offer feel inevitable and is legible in under a second.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: A relaxed model reclining, phone-in-scene running the Klarna app — lifestyle and product proof folded into one image, all in the pink family.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: The Klarna wordmark sits low-centre with a quiet 'Try it now' beneath it — no loud button color, trusting the brand and low-friction verb to carry the click.

principle.title: Write the headline as two parallel beats
principle.explanation: 'Shopping, done. Payments, later.' uses matched two-word rhythm so the value proposition lands in a single glance. Parallel structure makes copy feel inevitable and memorable, and it survives being read in the sub-second window a display ad actually gets.
principle.howToApply: When drafting a display unit or hero headline, try shaping the promise as two symmetrical fragments of the same length and grammar; let the repetition do the persuading, keep the palette monochrome around it, and fold your proof into the hero image instead of adding a separate element.

entries.tagline: The hand-typesetter who broke the Swiss grid from the inside — the father of New Wave (Swiss Punk) typography.
entries.summary: Weingart trained as a metal-type compositor, then taught at the Schule für Gestaltung Basel from 1968 alongside his own teachers Emil Ruder and Armin Hofmann. Rather than reject Swiss typography, he interrogated it rule by rule — wide letterspacing, layering, stepped lines, reversed type, photomechanical collage — and built New Wave out of the pieces. His American students (April Greiman among them) carried the language back to the US, reshaping graphic design there.
whatWorks: Manipulate the real material, not a mockup of it — discover form by pushing the medium's actual constraints (lead spacing, film, photostats)
Build a personal vocabulary of ~20–30 reusable moves you can recombine, instead of reinventing each layout
Invert one inherited rule at a time (spacing, grid, weight, tone) as a generative prompt
Earn the right to break a system by first mastering it by hand
Turn the tooling itself (copier, film overlays, crumpled tissue) into a source of form
Keep legibility as the tension the experiment plays against — not an obstacle to ignore

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: At the composing stick, not the sketchbook. Every experiment started hands-on in metal type and photomechanical process — he manipulated the actual material (letterspacing with lead, film positives, photostats) rather than drawing an idea of a layout.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Systematic rule-breaking. He took each 'law' of Swiss style (tight setting, the strict grid, a single weight) and asked what happens if you invert it — wide-spacing letters, stepping lines, layering type over image — assembling a recombinable vocabulary of moves rather than one-off tricks.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Photomechanical collage — overlapping film positives, photocopying crumpled tissue for texture, reversing type out of tonal fields. He turned the darkroom and the copier into compositional tools decades before Photoshop made layering ordinary.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: Master the craft before you subvert it — his freedom rested on a typesetter's apprenticeship. Break a rule to add meaning or emotion, never for novelty, and hold legibility as the counter-tension so the disruption still reads as typography.

principle.title: Master the grid by hand before you break it
principle.explanation: New Wave wasn't chaos: it was a trained compositor subverting rules he knew intimately. The discipline underneath is exactly what kept the disruption meaningful rather than merely messy — the breakage reads as intent because the underlying order is felt.
principle.howToApply: Before you deviate from a design system, rebuild its rules once yourself — set the type on the grid, hit every token. Then break exactly one rule at a time as a deliberate, legible move, so an 'expressive' layout still communicates. See [[april-greiman]] for how a student carried this into digital.

entries.tagline: A magazine that treats its homepage like a printed cover — one issue, two actions, and vast quiet space doing the luxury work.
entries.summary: Kinfolk applies print-magazine logic to the web: the landing is essentially the current issue's cover, centred on the page with only 'Buy' and 'Read' as choices, floating in an ocean of warm off-white. Below that calm hero, content arrives as restrained image-led article rows — tiny sans labels, letterspaced serif titles, honest editorial photography — punctuated by a single high-contrast black subscription bar and a greige 'free preview' block. Nothing shouts; the emptiness itself reads as premium. The one caveat is that the homepage keeps loading into a very long article feed, so the disciplined top gives way to a more ordinary scroll.
whatWorks: Extreme negative space as the primary material: the fold is ~70% empty, which signals calm and confidence more than any decoration could
Print-cover logic — lead with ONE object (the issue cover) and only two verbs, 'Buy' and 'Read', instead of a menu of CTAs
Classic editorial type pairing: a letterspaced old-style serif for the masthead/headlines against a small quiet sans for kickers and deks
Image-led article cards where honest, un-stylised portrait/landscape photography carries the mood; text stays a whisper beneath
One deliberate high-contrast interruption — the slim black 'Subscribe Now' bar — so the single conversion moment stands out against all the white
A warm near-white (not pure #fff) plus muted greige section blocks keep the whole page soft and gallery-like

sections.name: Masthead / nav
sections.notes: Just the letterspaced serif 'KINFOLK' wordmark top-left and a hamburger top-right — the chrome gets out of the way so the issue can be the page.

sections.name: Hero — current issue
sections.notes: 'ISSUE 60 / HISTORY SPECIAL' centred in serif caps above the cover image, with a 1/10 carousel and 'Buy' / 'Read' flanking it. The homepage IS the cover; symmetry + whitespace make it feel like a physical object.

sections.name: Subscription bar
sections.notes: A slim full-width black band ('Discover unlimited access to Kinfolk' + outlined 'Subscribe Now'). The only loud element on the page, so the one revenue ask can't be missed.

sections.name: Featured articles row
sections.notes: A horizontal run of cards (Tove Jansson home tour, Cosima Vahesar, Rebecca Solnit, a garden piece): small 'Arts & Culture, Issue 60' kicker, caps title, one-line dek. Photography leads; copy is deliberately minimal.

sections.name: Free preview
sections.notes: 'FREE PREVIEW — Take a look inside Issue Sixty' on a muted greige block with two large image cards (David Chipperfield, 'Finding Albion'). The tonal background gently separates it without a hard divider.

sections.name: Newsletter + footer
sections.notes: A single understated line, 'Never miss out—sign up for newsletters from Kinfolk', with underline-only inputs, resolving into a dark image-led footer band. Restraint held all the way to the bottom.

principle.title: Make whitespace the loudest thing on the page.
principle.explanation: Premium and calm are communicated by what you remove, not what you add. When the fold is mostly empty and there's only one object and one or two actions, the eye has nowhere to go but the thing that matters — and the restraint itself reads as taste and confidence.
principle.howToApply: On a hero, strip it to one focal object plus at most two verbs, and let 60–70% of the first screen be empty warm-white. Reserve exactly one high-contrast element (a dark bar or button) for the single most important action so the quiet has one deliberate exception.

entries.tagline: One flat field of brand-red and three product states that literally show the promise — play handheld, tabletop, or on the TV.
entries.summary: This unit sells the single core benefit of the Switch — that it plays anywhere — by showing the exact same console in its three modes across one frame: handheld, tabletop with detached Joy-Cons, and docked to a TV. A saturated flat Nintendo-red background isolates the hardware so nothing competes with it, the headline states the benefit in five words, and a single pill CTA closes. It's textbook display craft: one idea, one colour, one action.
whatWorks: The product IS the proof — showing three play modes in one row demonstrates 'anywhere' faster than any copy could claim it
A single flat brand-red fills the whole field so the hardware pops with zero background noise and the brand colour is unmissable
Five-word benefit headline ('Play Together Anytime, Anywhere') in bold caps — readable in the half-second a banner gets
One clear pill CTA ('Offers On Now') in white, the only enclosed shape on the frame, so the click target is obvious
Left-locked logo + centred headline + product baseline create a clean left-to-right read with a stable horizon

sections.name: Hook
sections.notes: The wall of saturated Nintendo-red stops the scroll on colour alone, then the trio of familiar consoles pulls the eye across the frame.

sections.name: Message / headline
sections.notes: 'PLAY TOGETHER ANYTIME, ANYWHERE' — bold white caps, two tight lines, benefit-first and instantly legible against the red.

sections.name: Visual / hero
sections.notes: The same console shown in three modes (handheld, tabletop, docked-to-TV) is the whole argument; each state is spaced so it reads as a sequence, not clutter.

sections.name: CTA
sections.notes: A single white pill, 'OFFERS ON NOW', is the only outlined shape — low-key wording but unmistakable as the one thing to click.

principle.title: Let the product demonstrate the benefit instead of claiming it.
principle.explanation: A banner has a half-second to land one idea. Showing the product in the exact states that embody the promise (here, three ways to play) proves the claim visually and needs almost no reading — far stronger than an adjective-laden headline.
principle.howToApply: For a feature-led ad, stage the product itself performing the benefit (before/after, or the key modes side by side) on a single flat brand colour, add a five-word benefit line, and one enclosed CTA — nothing else.

entries.tagline: Turned magazine typography into image — building bespoke letterforms and a visual system that made 'The Face' feel like a movement.
entries.summary: Brody rose to fame art-directing The Face in the 1980s, where he treated type as expressive image rather than neutral information, drawing on Dada, Futurism and Rodchenko. He built custom typefaces and a recurring visual grammar for each publication, later co-founding FontShop and the experimental FUSE project. His lasting lesson is method, not style: design a system and its own alphabet, then evolve it issue to issue so the whole feels authored.
whatWorks: Design a repeatable visual system (grid, symbols, type treatments) for a project, then vary it rather than restarting each time — consistency through evolution
Treat typography as image: letterforms can be the hero, not just the label
When existing type won't carry the idea, draw your own — a bespoke typeface (like his 'Industria' for The Face) makes a brand unmistakable
Mine unlikely sources (Dada, Constructivism, street signage) and translate their energy into a modern layout
Ignore inherited 'rules' deliberately, but keep legibility as the non-negotiable floor — experiment on top of a readable base
Build recurring symbols/marks that let readers navigate and recognise the publication at a glance

sections.name: How they begin
sections.notes: Starts from the publication's attitude, not a template — asks what the thing should feel like, then invents a visual language (marks, type, grid) specific to it, borrowing energy from Dada, Futurism and Rodchenko.

sections.name: Core method
sections.notes: Builds a system and then evolves it across issues: consistent underlying structure with deliberately changing type treatments, so the design feels authored and alive rather than fixed or generic.

sections.name: Signature technique
sections.notes: Type-as-image — widely letterspaced words, type set on diagonals/curves, dramatic size contrast, and hand-built custom typefaces (Industria, Typeface Six) drawn when off-the-shelf fonts couldn't carry the idea.

sections.name: Decision rules / philosophy
sections.notes: 'Design cannot remain neutral' — typography should express and provoke; break conventions on purpose, but never sacrifice the reader's ability to actually read it.

principle.title: Invent a system and its own alphabet — then evolve it, don't restart it.
principle.explanation: Brody's power came from authoring a visual language (grid + recurring marks + bespoke type) and then varying it issue to issue. That yields something that feels both consistent and alive, and makes a brand unmistakably its own instead of assembled from stock parts.
principle.howToApply: For a recurring project (newsletter, deck series, site sections), define a small kit — a grid, two type treatments, a couple of recurring symbols — and commit to remixing that same kit each time rather than redesigning; draw or heavily customise one signature type element so it can't be mistaken for anyone else's.
